Earnings Keep the Market Strong Despite Signs of Consumer Weakness | The Weekly Wrap

Steve Eisman(172.0K)·28:11·38.7K views·2026-05-01
Steve EismanPortfolio Manager, Neuberger Berman
TL;DRThe U.S.
economy remains bifurcated ("K-shaped"), where massive AI-driven infrastructure spending by Big Tech and industrials continues to propel the market upward despite clear evidence of a recession among low-to-middle-income consumers. The primary driver of current GDP...

TL;DR

The U.S. economy remains bifurcated ("K-shaped"), where massive AI-driven infrastructure spending by Big Tech and industrials continues to propel the market upward despite clear evidence of a recession among low-to-middle-income consumers.

The AI Arms Race and Industrial Beneficiaries

The primary driver of current GDP and market strength is the unabated surge in Capital Expenditure (CapEx) related to artificial intelligence.

  • Big Tech Earnings: Results from Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta confirm no slowdown in AI spending.
      • Google: Significant acceleration in cloud revenue ($20B) drove a 10% stock surge.
      • Microsoft: Azure grew 39%, though the stock softened as investors sought even higher beats.
      • Amazon: AWS revenue rose 28%, and the company reported $225B in revenue commitments for its "Trainium" AI chip.
      • Meta: Despite beating metrics, shares fell after the company raised its 2026 CapEx outlook to $135B, signaling a high-cost battle for AI dominance.
  • Industrial Infrastructure: The "data center boom" is fueling non-tech sectors.
      • Quanta Services: Benefiting from utility construction and maintenance required for AI power needs.
      • Caterpillar: Sales grew 38% in the construction unit, driven largely by data center equipment demand.

The K-Shaped Economy: Consumer Divergence

Earnings reports reveal a sharp divide between the financial health of different consumer segments.

  • Signs of Weakness:
      • Domino’s Pizza: Weak same-store sales (0.9% vs. 2.3% expected) suggest the low-to-middle-end consumer is currently in a "deep recession."
      • Starbucks: Despite a difficult backdrop, reported its second straight quarter of traffic growth, suggesting business changes are finally taking hold.
  • Signs of Strength:
      • Visa: Net revenue rose 17% with payment volumes up 9%, indicating that overall consumer spending remains robust at the higher end of the "K."
      • Eli Lilly: Reported a "blowout" quarter (EPS of $8.55 vs. $6.66 expected), confirming its dominance in the weight-loss drug market.

High-Conviction Plays and Market Risks

Steve Eisman detailed his specific thesis on several controversial or high-profile names.

  • Charter Communications (Conviction Long): Despite a 25% stock drop following a loss of 120,000 broadband subscribers, Eisman is holding and adding to his position.
      • Thesis: CapEx is scheduled to drop from $11B to ~$7.5B by 2028, which should theoretically drive massive free cash flow for stock buybacks.
  • FICO (Conviction Short): Eisman remains short, citing a massive pricing disparity between FICO and its competitor, VantageScore.
      • The Math: For every 100 applications, FICO charges ~$2,049 compared to VantageScore’s ~$99. Eisman believes lenders will inevitably shift toward the lower-cost provider once implementation begins in 2027.
  • Private Credit Concerns: Eisman questioned the "marks" on software loans within private credit portfolios (specifically Ares and Blue Owl).
      • Refinancing Risk: With public software valuations down significantly (ServiceNow used as a proxy), many private equity-backed software firms may find it impossible to refinance loans when their equity value has halved.

Geopolitics and Macro Trends

Global alliances and market concentrations are shifting, creating new layers of uncertainty.

  • The UAE’s Exit from OPEC: A "canary moment" signaling a shift in worldwide alliances. The UAE appears ready to "go it alone" to increase production and grab market share amid the Iran conflict.
  • Energy Prices: Brent crude has climbed above $115 as the U.S. maintains a blockage of the Strait of Hormuz to pressure Iran economically.
  • Market Concentration: Semiconductors now constitute 16% of the S&P 500. This makes the entire index hypersensitive to any negative news regarding AI or data center demand.

***

Notable Quotes

"When a pizza company shows weak same-store sales, it implies that the middle and low-end consumer is having a hard time... the bottom of the K consumers are in a deep recession." — Steve Eisman
"There is no slowdown in AI CapEx on the horizon. AI CapEx is what is driving GDP, and a strong GDP drives the stock market." — Steve Eisman
"Regime changes are notoriously difficult to predict. [Commentators] argued that stagflation had arrived... and yet, on this rally, the leaders were the same leaders of the last few years: tech stocks and banks." — Steve Eisman
The UAE made the shocking, shocking decision to remove itself from OPEC. It is also a canary moment as worldwide alliances shift and reform. Charter lost 120,000 broadband subscribers in 1Q 26. There is no way to sugarcoat the quarter. It was not a good one. Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta all reported. There is no slowdown in AI CapEx on the horizon. Never forget that we are living in a K-shaped economy, and that shows up in the results of companies like Domino's. When a pizza company shows weak same-store sales, it implies that the middle and low-end consumer is having a hard time. Hi, this is Steve Eisman, and welcome to another edition of the Weekly Wrap. This is for the week ending Friday, May 1, but recorded Thursday night, April 30th. I'm excited to share that in a few weeks, we'll be announcing an additional feature to The Real Eisman Playbook alongside our existing Monday and Friday episodes. Thank you to everyone who has signed up for the mailing list on The Real Eisman Playbook, and a confirmation email will be going out really soon inviting you to join the Founders Club. I want to emphasize that Monday and Friday episodes will stay accessible at no cost on YouTube and all audio channels. The new features are something we've been building behind the scenes, and we are excited to reveal them soon. The goal is to have more content and greater access to me and the community we are building together. Visit the link to The Real Eisman Playbook in this episode's description, or visit our website realeismanplaybook.com to sign up for our mailing list. As I mentioned before, signing up for the mailing list gives founder status when our new project goes live. Before we start the wrap, I want to address what happened to Charter on Friday of last week. On Thursday and Friday of last week, Comcast and Charter reported. The results that Comcast reported on Thursday were mixed but mostly okay, and the stock rallied. The results Charter reported on Friday were poor, and the stock declined 25%. Now, I recommended Charter in January at $223. The stock had a nice rally until Friday, but after Friday is now down 22% on the year. I did not discuss Charter last week because I wanted time to digest what happened and then give you all my thoughts. My goal in sharing my analysis is to provide total transparency. I continue to own the stock as I did when I made the recommendation, and I have not changed my thesis. Whether or not I am proven right or wrong, it's important to me to share how I think about this investment. I have said in the past that thesis creep is a major problem for investors, and I advise to always be aware of the tendency to change the thesis as the stock moves. In other words, have the tail wag the dog. My thesis, I want to emphasize, has not changed. First, let's quickly review the thesis. The thesis is part valuation and part turnaround. Over the past few years, Charter has embarked on major CapEx to enhance its systems, and that CapEx is ending now. And as a result, total CapEx will decline from 11 billion in 2026 to 9 and a half billion in 2027 and then 7 and a half to 8 billion in 2028 and 2029. That decline in CapEx will enhance free cash flow dollar for dollar. And the company will use that cash flow to buy back stock, with the possibility that the company will buy back literally 50% of its shares over the next four to five years. That is certainly doable given that the company's market cap is only 23 billion dollars. The stock is currently valued at a 2026 PE of four times. It is valued at less than two times free cash flow in those outer years. And it has an almost, get this, 70% free cash flow yield in those outer years as well. Given the valuation, the market either does not believe that Charter's CapEx will decline and does not believe that they will buy back stock with their free cash flow, or the market believes that EBITDA will somehow fall off a cliff. Now, I don't believe that those eventualities are possible. Yes, Charter is inexpensive. Ridiculously inexpensive. Priced as if it will eventually go bankrupt. But, and this is the important but, an inexpensive stock does not necessarily make a good investment. Stocks remain cheap for a long time. I thought that after the fourth quarter broadband results came in better than expected, Charter had started a real turnaround story. This quarter was, unfortunately, a setback. The data point that most investors focus on is changes in broadband subscribers. Charter lost 120,000 broadband subscribers in 1Q 26, which was worse than the 100,000 loss estimate and double last year's losses. There is no way to sugarcoat the quarter. It was not a good one. However, I believe that Charter is in a good position to turn the story around. The industry is moving to a world of convergence where cable and mobile phone service are offered as packages. Here, Charter has an advantage in that it is the low-cost provider. It offers cable and mobile for $100 a month versus 180 to 200 by its competitors. So, I think this is a stock worth sticking with, and I added to my position on Friday. I don't know when the turnaround will take place, but the stock is so inexpensive, in a sense, I am being paid to wait. And now moving on to this week's wrap. We will discuss the war in Iran, the UAE resigning from OPEC and what it could mean, comments on the post-March rally, a comment on private credit, and the huge, and I mean huge, week for earnings across multiple sectors. And we will discuss as many companies as we can so we can help ascertain the health of the U.S. economy. Not much news on the war. Over the weekend, there were no negotiations. Iran made a proposal to reopen the strait in exchange for ending the war and tabling negotiations on its nuclear program for later. President Trump, unsurprisingly, rejected the proposal but has not resumed bombing. It looks like the current U.S. strategy is to maintain the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz, thereby putting economic pressure on Iran. Because of this strategy, Brent crude oil prices have climbed above $115. Coincidence or not, the UAE made the shocking, shocking decision to remove itself from OPEC. Saudi Arabia controls pricing and production of OPEC, and the UAE is signaling not only that it has had enough—we've known for years that they strained under the constraints placed on them—but now they are signaling their willingness to go it alone and believe in their own strength. Possibly they are signaling a shift in alliances behind the scenes, and this will continue to play out. It is also a canary moment as worldwide alliances shift and reform. We truly can't handicap this momentous political economic decision and the impact on oil and pricing. But at first glance, it seems like the UAE goal is to increase production, take advantage of the war, and grab market share. We will have to wait for the behind the scenes motivations to be revealed. The market has had a big move since the end of March, and it's really worth commenting on. At the end of March, the S&P and NASDAQ were down 4% and 7% respectively for the year. But as of Thursday of this week, the S&P is now up 5% for the year, and NASDAQ is up 7% for the year. Quite a rally. And this rally feels similar to what happened last year. If you recall last year, the market had a big correction from late February through April 9th. And thereafter, the market staged a rally which kept going with fits and starts through year end. This year looks similar, almost to the exact date. What's intriguing to me is that during the recent market correction, there were several commentators who were calling for a regime change. They argued that stagflation had arrived. "Sell your index funds," they said. "Sell your tech stocks. Buy energy and staples," they said. And yet, on this rally, the leaders were the same leaders of the last few years: tech stocks and banks. And energy stocks and staples lagged. Regime changes are notoriously difficult to predict. Two additional points. First, gold has been acting weirdly. Normally, gold acts as a hedge against inflation and the demise of fiat currency. It should have rallied during the war, but it did nothing and is still doing nothing. I'm not sure what this means, but it's worth watching. Second, semiconductors are now, get this, 16% of the S&P 500, an all-time high, which means it constitutes 46% of the entire info-tech sector. In contrast, software is now only 8% of the S&P, down from its peak of 12% in August 2025. The high percentage of the S&P by semis also means that the overall market is easily swayed by any positive or negative news with respect to AI. On Tuesday of this week, there was an article in the Wall Street Journal that OpenAI missed its revenue targets. It was also reported that OpenAI's CFO is nervous that OpenAI will not be able to meet its massive data center commitments. That one news story caused a one-day correction in the semis subsector, the info-tech sector, and NASDAQ. This coming Monday, I will explore all of these market movements with Chris Verrone, Chief Market Strategist at Strategas, and Todd Sohn, Chief Chartist at Strategas. So, check it out. Before we get to earnings, there was an interesting story in private credit. Ares Management, a publicly traded alternative asset manager, symbol ARES, Ares, owns a publicly traded private credit fund called Ares Capital Corp, symbol ARCC, which has 29 and a half billion in assets. Ares hired an outside consulting firm to examine ARCC's software exposure. According to this outside consulting firm, 85% of ARCC's software loans are at low risk, 14% are at medium risk, and 1% is at high risk. The 14% and 1% problematic software loans equate to 3.3% of the entire portfolio, or 1 billion. Now, I have a problem with the way Ares categorizes these loans. When they say that 85% of the software exposure is low risk, what exactly does that mean? Let's assume that means that the companies are performing fine right now. That's really not the issue. Let's use ServiceNow as a proxy for the sector. In July 2025, ServiceNow was priced at $209 a share. It's now below 90, a decline of 60%. That implies that the value of all private equity software companies is less than half the purchase price. The equity values of software companies in ARCC's loan portfolio that are supposedly doing well are still worth less than half the original purchase price. Who is going to refinance the loans when the equity value is less than half? That is the key question. And now onto earnings. Since we're talking about private equity, let's start with Blue Owl, which reported Thursday morning. The stock was up 10% on the print because the numbers were not a disaster, and a 19% of the float is short. I'd highlight two things. First, Owl reported a negative 40 basis point return for the quarter for direct lending. This is the area that investors are focused on. And like ARCC, the question is not about the current marks, but who is going to refinance software loans when the equity is worth less than half. The other thing I'd flag is that Owl's equity-based comp increased 21% quarter over quarter and 15% year over year to $196 million, coming in 50% above consensus estimates of 130 million. That's a lot of stock-based comp to the management of a company whose stock is down 39% just this year alone. One more thing on stock-based comp. The entire sector of alternative asset managers reports adjusted earnings by adding back stock-based comp. Blue Owl too. But Blue Owl uses a ton of stock-based comp to such a degree that under GAAP accounting, the company is barely profitable. Next up is Domino's Pizza. While a recession does not seem imminent at all, never forget that we are living in a K-shaped economy, and that shows up in the results of companies like Domino's. On Monday, Domino's reported and the stock was down 8%. The key metric, the key metric for any retailer, is same-store sales. Unfortunately, Domino's same-store sales were up only 0.9% versus the expected 2.3%. And earnings per share were down 5% versus last year. When a pizza company shows weak same-store sales, it implies that the middle and low-end consumer is having a hard time. Remember, the market is amoral. Domino's earnings are telling us that the bottom of the K consumers are in a deep recession. The fact that the market continues to trend up shows the disconnect between consumers and market movements. Buy the dip will be the mantra until proven otherwise. And as long as AI CapEx infrastructure spending pulls all the weight of GDP growth, the market seems generally willing to reflect the good news and discount the sad news. General Motors. GM shows how well a stock can do when it is inexpensive and has a turnaround story. It's what I'm hoping for with Charter. In November 2023, GM stock price was $27. It's now 78. What happened? The company started to perform. Let's just look briefly at this quarter. GM reported its first quarter results, which saw beats across the board. The company continues to navigate a difficult electronic vehicle backdrop. Total revenue came in at 43.6 billion, slightly above the street's estimate, despite seeing a decline in its U.S. automotive sales in the quarter of 10% year over year. Earnings per share came in at 3.70, well above the street of 2.60 and versus 2.78 last year. And the company raised guidance. Moving on. Starbucks. This has been a turnaround story that stubbornly refused to turn around until perhaps this quarter. Starbucks reported its second straight quarter of traffic growth. EPS was 50 cents versus 42 cents expected and versus 41 cents last year. Revenue also beat. Same-store sales growth rose an impressive 6.2%, largely because of North America. It also raised its full-year outlook. Impressive. And the stock was up 5% after hours on Tuesday night, and up over 8% by the end of day on Wednesday, and flat at the end of day Thursday. Investors are finally rewarding the company's business changes. I think the takeaway is that inflection points are hard to predict because real corporate changes take time to implement, and catching any bottom or top at exactly the right moment is very difficult. Now let's talk about Visa. Yes, it is a K-shaped economy. But overall, consumer spending is still strong. At least that is what Visa's results indicate. Visa's net revenue was up 17% versus last year, and that is the biggest percentage increase since 2022. Earnings per share of 3.31 was a beat and versus an expectation of 3.10 and up an impressive 20% versus last year. The all-important payment volume metric was up a strong 9%. Visa, in my view, gives as good a read on the consumer as any company in the world. For now, the picture that has emerged is that the consumer is still spending, but the middle and lower end consumer are having a hard time. Hence, the weak results at Domino's Pizza. Full disclosure, I've owned Visa for years. FICO. Fair Isaac. This is a very controversial name, and full disclosure, I have been short for several months. Two Mondays from now, we will host a sell-side analyst who covers this subsector, and we will examine the FICO issues in depth. FICO reported Tuesday night. The stock was up over 10% after hours Tuesday night but faded on Wednesday, closing up only 3.4%. Now, FICO beat and raised guidance. On the call, management also said that it does not expect to lose mortgage market share to VantageScore, the score created by the three credit bureaus. Losing or not losing mortgage market share to VantageScore will determine the fate of this stock. Here's why I think management's prediction that it will not lose share could be terribly wrong. In the next few months, the FHFA, the regulator of Fannie and Freddie Mac, will give final approval to FICO's new Score 10 T and to VantageScore. Those are the two products that will compete head-to-head. FICO will charge for Score 10 T 99 cents per loan application plus $65 per funded loan. The three credit bureaus will charge only 99 cents per loan application for VantageScore. This is a tremendous pricing difference. Here's why. On average, lenders will fund approximately 30% of their mortgage applications. So, for every 100 applications, FICO collects 99 cents times 100 plus 65 times 30, which is $99 plus $1,950 or $2,049 total. Vantage collects 99 cents times 100, that's it, or $99 total. So, for every 100 loan applications, the pricing is 2,049 for FICO versus $99 for VantageScore. Now, that's quite an enormous pricing difference. As for timing, both FICO Score 10 T and VantageScore are being pilot programmed this year with full implementation scheduled for 2027. The crazy part of this story is that FICO is a monopoly, but to generate its score, it pulls data from the three credit bureaus who are now its competitor. It's one thing to anger your competitors. It's another thing to anger your competitors by making money off their hard work when they are your suppliers. Moving on. Bookings. A very good company. But last week, United Airlines and American Airlines lowered 2026 guidance because of the war. This week, Bookings reported and did the same thing. The actual reported results were fine with EPS growth of 15%. However, for the June quarter, the company cut its revenue growth outlook to 4 to 6% versus the consensus of 11%, and the stock was down on this report. Enphase. This is a solar stock I have spoken about before. It sells solar microinverters and battery storage systems to consumers. The consumer solar side of the business has been in something of a depression for the past few years, as is evident from Enphase's stock price, which has declined from $335 at the end of 2022 to its current 33. Every quarter, investors hope that the business has bottomed and that revenue growth will resume. They were disappointed again. Earnings per share was 47 cents versus 68, a decline of 31%. The company guided second quarter revenue to 280 to 310 million, which is in line with expectations but provides no sign that business is improving. The stock lacks a thesis, and without one, it's hard to see why the stock will move up. Wednesday night was a big night. Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta all reported. On Thursday, Google was up 10%, Amazon was up 1%, Microsoft was down 4%, and Meta was down 8 and a half percent. Here's why. Google. Just a really powerful quarter. Google's total revenue was 94.7 billion. I'm going to say that again. For the quarter, Google's total revenue was 94.7 billion versus 91.6 billion expected. Google's cloud computing revenue of 20 billion versus 18 billion expected saw meaningful acceleration in growth. And that's all you really need to know. Microsoft. Revenue was 54.5 billion versus 53.8 billion expected. That's good. Earnings per share was 4.27 versus 4.05 expected and versus 3.46 last year, which equates to a very impressive 23% growth, also good. And most importantly, its cloud computing business Azure saw 39% revenue growth versus 38% expected. And the company gave strong guidance on the call. So why was the stock down? Well, the 39% Azure revenue growth was only 1% better than expected, and I think some investors were looking for more. On such data, stocks move. Amazon posted a nice beat on earnings per share of 2.78 versus a dollar 63 expected. Revenue of 181.5 billion exceeded the estimate of 177.3 billion. Most importantly, Amazon Web Services cloud unit revenue rose 28% versus last year. That is better than last quarter's 24% and better than the 26% estimate. On the call, the CEO said that the company now has over 225 billion in revenue commitments for Trainium, AWS's AI chip. And that news really excited investors, and the stock moved from being down after hours to up 1% on Thursday. Meta. Nothing wrong with the quarter. The company beat on most metrics. That was not the issue. CapEx was the issue. Meta raised its 2026 CapEx outlook to 135 billion from 125 billion. And investors, I guess, have grown tired of Meta continuously upping its AI CapEx budget. Meta's problem is that even at 135 billion, it is being outspent by Google, Microsoft, and Amazon. Main takeaway from the results of these big four: there is no slowdown in AI CapEx on the horizon. AI CapEx is what is driving GDP, and a strong GDP drives the stock market. Moving on. Eli Lilly. I also have owned this stock for years. And there is no question about it, Lilly has won the diet drug wars. Lilly issued a blowout, a blowout quarter. Earnings per share of 8.55 versus 6.66 expected and versus 3.34 last year. Wow. Revenue was 19.8 billion versus 17.6 billion expected. Lilly raised revenue and EPS guidance for the year. Let's move on to two industrial names. The industrial sector has become bifurcated. Companies that are somehow AI-related are doing well; everything else is somewhat lagging. Last week, we focused on GEV and its gas turbine story. This week, let's look at Quanta and Caterpillar. First, Quanta. Full disclosure, I've owned this stock for years as well. It's the company that utilities use for construction and maintenance. It reported another great quarter. Earnings per share was 2.68 versus a dollar 78 last year and versus the estimate of only 2.08, so quite a big beat. And revenue of 7.87 billion was up 26% versus last year and better than the estimate of 7 billion. Powerful numbers because Quanta is a big beneficiary of the AI data construction boom, which requires more electricity from utilities. Caterpillar. Now, you might not think that Caterpillar is an AI-related story, but it is. All that data center construction requires equipment from companies like Cat. And Cat posted earnings per share of 5.54 versus 4.25 last year and versus the 4.63 estimate, so a very nice beat. And revenue of 17.4 billion was up 22% and beat the estimate by more than a billion. Most importantly, sales grew 38% in the construction industries unit, the division most related to AI CapEx. And finally, Apple reported Thursday night. It was a good quarter but not a great quarter. Earnings per share was 2.01 versus a dollar 65 last year, 22% growth and a beat versus expectations. Apple reported total revenue growth of 17%, which was also a beat. The only negative, and it's not a small one, is that sales for iPhone missed estimates for the second time in three quarters. This last Monday, we hosted an interview with Chris Edson, Global Head of Originations at Apollo Global Management. The controversy surrounding private credit continues to dominate headlines, and Chris has a front row seat as to what is really going on in the private credit private equity sector and how Apollo is navigating these waters. It's a great interview, and I think you will learn a lot. So, check it out. This coming Monday, we will post an interview with Chris Verrone, head strategist at Strategas, and Todd Sohn, head chartist at Strategas. We discuss the recent rally in the market, what's working and what is not, and any and all pitfalls to avoid. So, please tune in. Be sure to check out our website realeismanplaybook.com. If you're enjoying these weekly wraps and our podcast, we kindly ask that you support the channel by subscribing to our YouTube channel and to our audio channels, The Real Eisman Playbook. Subscribing is the best way to help us expand this community to more like-minded individuals such as yourselves, and we greatly appreciate your support. And that's the wrap.

Beyond Bigger Models: Recursion As The Next Scaling Law In AI

Y Combinator(2.2M)·37:53·3.7K views·2026-05-01
Ankit GuptaY Combinator Francois ChaubardY Combinator
TL;DR"It is actually impossible for the [standard LLM] model to map from an unsorted list to a sorted list on a one-shot…
Recursive AI models like Hierarchical Reasoning Models (HRM) and Tiny Recursive Models (TRM) achieve state-of-the-art reasoning performance—outperforming models 1,000x their size on tasks like the ARC Prize—by utilizing weight reuse and latent memory states at inference...

TL;DR: Recursive AI models like Hierarchical Reasoning Models (HRM) and Tiny Recursive Models (TRM) achieve state-of-the-art reasoning performance—outperforming models 1,000x their size on tasks like the ARC Prize—by utilizing weight reuse and latent memory states at inference time to bypass the "reasoning ceiling" of standard feed-forward LLMs.

The Structural Limits of Standard LLMs

  • Feed-Forward Constraints: While modern LLMs excel at next-token prediction, they are essentially one-shot feed-forward processes at training time. This limits their ability to solve "incompressible" problems that require iterative computation.
  • The Sorting Analogy: Francois Chaubard explains that a transformer with 30 layers cannot solve a comparison sort for a 31-element list because the problem has a theoretical lower bound of $N \log N$ steps. Standard models literally "run out of layers" to complete the necessary logic.
  • Discrete vs. Latent Reasoning:
      • Chain of Thought (CoT): This is a "hack" that forces recursion in the visible token space, but it is limited by human-labeled data and training traces.
      • Recursive Latent Space: True recursive models reason within a continuous, high-dimensional latent space (memory), making them more expressive and efficient than models that must "snap back" to discrete tokens.
  • The Einstein Test: Standard models are bounded by the limits of human knowledge. They can use tools (like a Python sort function) because they have seen them before, but they struggle to discover new algorithms (e.g., discovering Merge Sort if they only knew Bubble Sort).

Hierarchical Reasoning Models (HRM)

  • Architecture: Inspired by the human brain's varying processing frequencies, HRM uses three nested levels of recursion:
      • Low-level (L-net): Operates at high frequency for local features.
      • High-level (H-net): Operates at a lower frequency for broader context.
      • Outer Refinement Loop: Scales the reasoning depth at inference time.
  • The "Stop Grad" Innovation: To avoid the "vanishing gradient" problem common in old Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), HRM uses a fixed-point iteration method.
      • Memory Persistence: The model performs 16 iterations but only backpropagates through a small portion, essentially treating different memory states as a "mini-batch" rather than resetting activations to zero.
  • Performance: With only 27 million parameters and no pre-training, HRM achieved ~70% on the ARC Prize, while massive models like o3 initially scored zero.

Tiny Recursive Models (TRM): Complexity through Simplification

  • Model Efficiency: TRM refines the HRM approach by collapsing the dual networks into a single shared-weight transformer layer. Despite having only 7 million parameters, it outperforms HRM.
  • Architectural Shifts:
      • Weight Sharing: TRM uses the same network for both high and low-level features, proving that variable scoping (distinct memory states) is more important than having distinct physical layers.
      • Deep Supervision: It utilizes "truncated backprop through time," backpropagating through one full recursive loop rather than a single step, which significantly boosts performance.
  • Task Specificity: While small, these models are currently task-specific. A TRM trained for Sudoku cannot inherently solve ARC puzzles, marking a departure from the general-purpose nature of LLMs.

The Future of Scaling: Depth over Width

  • Compute Depth: The "next scaling law" involves increasing compute depth (recursion steps) rather than just parameter width.
  • Merging Paradigms: The ultimate frontier in AI research is combining the representation power of giant foundation models (like Gemini or GPT-4) with the recursive reasoning engines of TRMs.
  • Latent Reasoning: Future architectures will likely move reasoning into the latent "embedding" space rather than relying on the inefficient, token-by-token Chain of Thought method.

Notable Quotes

"It is actually impossible for the [standard LLM] model to map from an unsorted list to a sorted list on a one-shot basis... if I have a list that's 31 elements long and my transformer is 30 [layers], I run out of steps to do comparisons." — Francois Chaubard
"This is why this paper is so exciting: [TRM] is a 7-million parameter model that can solve what a trillion-parameter model can't solve, even when the latter is trained on the entire internet." — Francois Chaubard
"In the case of RNNs in general, they remain in this continuous latent space which is much, much higher dimensional... It’s much more expressive to be in continuous space, but we couldn't train it that way because you're inhibited by backprop through time." — Ankit Gupta
"We found mapping from pixels to some really cool latent space where things are nicely semantically separated... But now in that space, use these tiny reasoning models—use some type of recursion inside that—and I think that’s going to really work." — Francois Chaubard
Welcome back to another episode of Decoded. Today, I'm back with YC Visiting Partner Francois Chaubard to talk about one of the most interesting recent trends in AI research: recursion. Specifically, we're going to talk about how we can improve a model's reasoning performance by using recursion at inference time, rather than by just making the model bigger and bigger. There were two papers that made the power of this approach really clear in 2025. One on Hierarchical Reasoning Models, or HRM, and another on Tiny Recursive Models, or TRM. Francois, thanks for joining us. Can you tell us a little bit about these two models and what was so interesting about them? Sure. I guess, to set up a little bit of a foundation, you already did an amazing lecture on RNNs and LLMs in one of the previous videos, so I won't overdo it. Just to give the cliff notes, an RNN is just a model that you recursively call again and again and again on itself. We were very much in the belief that this was required to get to AGI. Peak RNN use was probably until 2016 with Alex Graves' NeurIPS keynote, which is just fantastic, and all his adaptive compute time work. So this is about 10 years ago that people were working on these models. This was in the era of LSTMs and LSTMs with attention. And, depending on which professors you talk to, this was before attention was invented. Yes, totally. And I think what really was the limiting step on RNNs in general was this thing called backprop through time, where you have to roll out the model, and then to update the weights, you need to approximate the gradient, and you step back, back, back, and you keep rolling out. As the model gets bigger and bigger, and as you roll out for more and more steps, then you have all these accumulation of errors. The gradient gets noisier and noisier, and then it just kind of stops working. Yeah, so you have these vanishing or exploding gradient problems. It's because if you have an input with 20 steps, you're multiplying these matrices 20 times and that causes training problems. And we're talking about doing context lengths of like a million or like a billion. So it's not even just 20; it's like a billion. Right. And even worse, you have to retain the activations at every single step. So if this were happening in your brain, you would need like a million copies of your brain at every single activation so that I can backprop through it. There's tricks around this that you can do, like gradient checkpointing and things like that to reduce that issue, but then you're just trading off memory for wall clock time and compute. Right, so now if you contrast that with LLMs, the ones that people are widely using, while at face value they appear to be similar, at training time they're doing basically this one-shot feed-forward process for every input, right? The LLM, the transformer block, can take all the inputs in parallel. It's not actually iteratively going over them one at a time at train time, so you don't have this problem of needing to store tons of activations or this giant vanishing gradients problem. Yeah, exactly. Like, it's actually all happening in time in one shot magically. That was the lower triangle trick that kind of happens with this causal mask. And so you actually do all time steps in one shot, and you forward pass a feed-forward model on all time steps in one shot and you back-wards in one shot. It's amazing for train time in terms of wall clock. It requires a lot of FLOPs and it still requires a lot of the memory. You still need it there, but you don't have the vanishing gradient issue. And what you actually paid for that you have to give up is this latent reasoning thing and this compression in the time direction. There is no compression in LLMs. Every single decode that I do, I still have to retain the entire Shakespeare novel just to decode a little bit. In RNNs you don't have to do that; it's all compressed in this hidden state that you kind of roll out. Okay, so let's talk about that in a little bit more detail. You refer to this inherent reasoning ability. Many people think about LLMs as doing reasoning, and we're going to talk about that a little bit later, but help me understand where you see the biggest limitations in LLM reasoning ability in terms of what the model does in an actual forward pass. Yeah, so I guess we go back to GPT-2. GPT-2 was this landmark architecture and paper that basically was just "get next token, next token, next token," and it kind of worked. We just watched val loss go down, perplexity go down, and the model just becomes more performant. It looks better and starts to make some Shakespeare that actually sounds somewhat plausible. Right. And then we have to get these things to reason and to actually solve some really hard problems. And I've done extensive experiments on this, but if you take, for example, sort. You have infinite amounts of unsorted lists and you give it sorted lists. You keep feeding it to the model, so it should work, right? It's actually impossible for the model to map from an unsorted list to a sorted list in a one-shot basis. It's literally that we know a theoretical lower bound that for comparison sort you can't do better than N log N steps. And if I have a list that's 31 characters or elements long, and my transformer is 30, I run out of steps to do comparisons. It's not possible for me to do all the steps that need to be done. In HRM and TRM they use Sudoku as an incompressible problem, and so are mazes. Rolling sum is another incompressible problem. When you mentioned the sorting algorithm, when I think back to my algorithms class from college, the one way you could get faster than N log N in a sorting algorithm is if you had some access to an external memory cache. If you had some tape you could write to, then you can actually do faster than N log N by basically selectively putting things into this memory. I suspect that's a key limitation of these LLMs, in that because there's no external memory tape inbuilt into the model, you lose certain performance possibilities in terms of how fast you can go. That's right. And so I guess radix sort would be the most common one. Depending on the number of buckets that you have, you can kind of get from N log N to order N. You can't get less than N; you have to touch all the elements. And if you run out of transformer layers in your neural network, then you ran out of chances to do that. Yeah, so this is just like going back to Alan Turing and a Turing machine, right? What's the analogy there exactly that we should think about in terms of LLMs not quite satisfying how you think about a Turing machine? Well, if we just talk about GPT-2, the original, no bells and whistles version, it's just a feed-forward model. And so it's just forward passing one step and taking an input, creating a bunch of outputs. In the Sudoku case, if I have 50 different potential steps and it's provable that I can only do one given this information, but I only have this many layers, then that's all I can do. And the cheat is the chain of thought. It's completely true that at test time, they are Turing complete and you can simulate all Turing computable functions. But how do you get it to learn it? You need to train it. And that's where, unless you're training it on human-labeled traces, for which there's a lot of problems like the Millennium Prize problem, we don't have the trace for it. Right, we'd love to have the trace for it, but it just doesn't exist. Totally. Makes sense. Okay, so with that context in mind, let's talk about these two papers, because I think that sets up a lot of the contrast we're going to draw between these papers and the models that people are maybe more used to. Let's talk about HRMs first. Walk me through a little bit about how this model works and some of the intuition behind it. Sure. So, this is directly in the lineage of RNNs. There's not that much novel from the RNN standpoint, at least in my opinion. They do have this idea, inspired by the brain, where you have different parts of the brain that operate on different frequencies. There's some that operate at a really high frequency, which is on the low level of the hierarchy, and some that operate at a really low frequency, which is the higher level of the hierarchy. The interplay between those things is really interesting. So this is like literally in the human brain; there's some bio-inspiration here, where you have different waves running at different frequencies at different parts of the brain or something like that. Okay, cool. Yeah. And I guess that interpretation is one way they are talking about classifying these hierarchies of frequencies. The most interesting part, at least for me, is the way that they train the neural network. You take in some X, some input, whether it's an incomplete Sudoku puzzle, a maze, or an ARC Prize challenge. You do TL steps with the L, the low-level module, then you go to H and you do that TH times, and then you have N sup outer refinement steps. Yeah, so you basically are running through the input with a given matrix, with a given transformation repeatedly on it. And you're doing that through two levels of refinement and then basically running that process several times. Yes, so then there's exactly three levels of recursion occurring here. There's the low level, there's the high level, and then there's the outer refinement steps. And we're calling it recursion because it's the same weights that are being applied repeatedly. We're not changing the weights in between these steps. Exactly right. You get to recurse on the L-net TL times, you recurse on the TH and the TL, this looped recursion TH times, and then you do this whole outer refinement step N sup times. Cool. And so what's the basic intuition for why that works? Why does that produce an effective paper result, and what even were the results that this paper showed? Yeah, so I mean this got state-of-the-art on ARC Prize 1 and 2. This was only a 27 million parameter model that was only trained on ARC Prize. So it's like a thousand inputs, like puzzles basically. Yeah, it's literally a thousand tasks, which is extremely small. There is no pre-training at all. This starts from literally tabula rasa weights, and it can outperform models like o3, if you remember back way back when. o3 gets zero, literally zero, and this got like something like 70% on ARC Prize 1 at least, which was just a huge breakthrough. And so you can think of this as variable scoping. If I have three nested functions, I guess the lowest level function has scoped variables, which they'll call Z L, which is the carry that inits at zero. A latent variable, basically. And like in traditional RNN literature they would call this the hidden state, the low-level hidden state. I get to recurse, recurse, recurse, and then I pass back that Z L back to the outer scoped function, the high-level one. I let that one do one iteration, it goes back and calls the low-level again, and it does this whole thing in a third outer loop, which is called the outer refinement step. Okay, but when you describe it like that, it seems like it would have the same backprop through time problem that you would have with RNNs. And I think they came up with a clever trick to basically get around that. So what was that trick that they figured out? This is really the crux of the paper that differentiates it. Instead of doing what Alex Graves did in all of his papers, from neural Turing machines to adaptive compute time to differentiable neural computers, where he always backpropped through all of the recursion steps, they do something different. He was limited by backprop through time, so he could only make the model so big and had issues with vanishing gradients. What they do is they kind of have this DEQ method of doing fixed-point iteration. So that's like deep equilibrium models, yeah cool. Yeah, deep equilibrium learning, where you take a batch and do something completely counterintuitive. As a computer vision person you'd never do this, but it actually does make sense. If I take a batch of like ImageNet and I forward pass through the model and I get some loss and I backprop and I update the weights, I would go get a different batch for the next one. But what they do instead is they actually do that 16 times. As you do that, you actually can see the change in your residuals get less and less. Why it actually makes sense is because in the RNN case, the Z L and the Z H, which are the carry, start out as zeros. Then we go through this whole loopy recursion through the two lower loops, and then I backprop just through the two modules, just once. I don't recurse all the way back; I do a stop grad. I stop right there, and then there's a huge residual, and then I don't reset Z L and Z H. I do it again at a different point in the carry or hidden variable space. So one can actually look at it as a different batch every time, even though it's the same exact X. Yeah, like the way I kind of think about it is like the 16 iterations or whatever that you're recursing over. It's like constructing a mini-batch, not from different inputs, but from different memory states basically. Right, it's like across this hidden or carry memory access. And that math holds and it works. It follows DEQ directly, in the event that the delta in Z L and Z H go to zero. It actually doesn't do that. And so we'll get to TRM, but Alexia basically shows that this is just not the case and you can't actually apply this math. That's not sufficient support for why it's working. We actually don't know why it's really working, and she figures out that you actually can backprop through all the way to the deep recursion, which actually improves performance much, much more. Interesting. Okay, so before we get into TRM, on this paper, I think there's a bunch of different ways people have looked at this in terms of why this may or may not be working. One is the sort of bio-plausibility argument. Now as you know, I'm usually not super keen on these. I think machine learning has a long history of people starting with bio-plausible arguments and then realizing that there's some variant that seems highly bio-implausible that actually works better. I think you have examples along these lines. Yeah, I mean, the classic first deep learning paper that started this whole craziness is AlexNet. In AlexNet there's actually this funny little thing called local receptive activation or depression, where once this activation fires, then I have this refractory region. It actually didn't work at all. It didn't work and you didn't need that. Then VGG came out and said get rid of all that, just go deeper with 3x3 convs, and it actually just outperforms dramatically. So this is always the case. Maybe you need to do it to get accepted into NeurIPS, add some biological plausibility. Sure, totally. You're definitely the expert here, but what do you consider to be bio-plausible and what's not? Well, I think that a lot of machine learning literature has overlapped a lot with people working in neuroscience, and I think it is very natural for us to ask questions about how our brain works. Because our brain is like an incredible instrument that does a ton of computing obviously, and does it in a very shockingly efficient manner. So a lot of machine learning research has for a long time sought analogs from how we think our brain works and tried to encode that in various systems. From the very basic concept of what a neural network is, it's called a neural network because we think it's some basic model for what a neuron is. Do you think that's a misnomer? The thing about them is that often we use bio-plausibility to inspire us to come up with ideas, but we end up veering away from the bio-plausible to something adjacent to them that is likely bio-implausible but seems to work better. And I think that runs better on a GPU. Exactly, it runs better on a GPU; it's more efficient in some capacity that is relevant to how we actually encode it. So I find thinking about bio-plausibility fun and interesting, and it's definitely a great way to inspire us to think about new things, but I tend to not be bounded by bio-plausibility when I think about what systems we should prioritize. I think the version of this that I find more compelling is actually that original discussion around automata theory and honestly just fundamental data structures and algorithms theory. If you're running a complex algorithm, having access to a sort of memory cache is actually very useful for being able to run that algorithm efficiently. And I kind of think of this set of hidden states as akin to a Turing machine tape or akin to the radix sort memory bank where you can basically train a model to use this memory cache in an intelligent way. Yeah, I think the point I wanted to make earlier is that we did this COT stuff and this tool use thing as ways to get beyond the limitations of GPT-2. The way that we get, you can actually—I've done this experiment—if you give me infinite amounts of unsorted lists and sorted lists, if I can do chain of thought and I can do every single step and teach it to do every single step, then I can actually get it to do sort and become a Turing machine at test time. And similarly, an even cheaper one that is much easier to do is you teach it and you say, "Hey, there's this Python function called sort. Just call the function." Just call the function, and that's the easiest thing to do and you don't need backprop at all. And so those are the two hacks. Now, well Francois, this is solved, like we're done, right? No, because I needed to know what sort was. What happens if we didn't know what merge sort was? So the chain of thought is not going to inherently discover sorting from first principles; it's finding it from our historical knowledge of everything it's trained on. Yeah, I mean, Demis has this whole thing about how the ultimate test is the Einstein test. Like go back to 1911 and then have it rebuild all the physics up until now. Similarly, let's just pretend that we only had bubble sort. If you chain of thoughted it on all the bubble sort input and output, it will only do bubble sort. In fact, it won't even do bubble sort that well. And then the tool use of course, it can only know bubble sort. I want to get to merge sort. How do I discover merge sort? I think the interesting thing just to emphasize here is there already exists some type of recursion that people are used to in LLMs, which is chain of thought, but that is a recursion that's happening in the token space of the model's outputs, not inherent to the model itself. And that's sort of the fundamental limitation: the model can only do a feed-forward one-shot output. We basically just have this hack where if you keep letting it output things, then it can read its outputs and do somewhat intelligent-seeming things, but it seems to be upper-bounded by the data that we feed it that the labs are very hungrily buying right now and not this sort of inherent underlying recursive reasoning. Yeah, so in both cases, in both hacks, you're bounded by the bounds of human knowledge. In the event it's outside the set of human knowledge, then you're kind of SOL. And so that's one. The other, you make a great point about discrete versus latent space. Reasoning in a discrete space can only output the carry in the case of LLMs where it has to be snapped back to some discrete token space. In the case of RNNs in general, they remain in this continuous latent space which is much higher dimensional. If you give me a tape that's this long and you cut it up into ten buckets versus all the possible values, it's much more expressive to be in continuous space. But we can't train it that way because you're inhibited by backprop through time largely. And this is why this paper is so exciting. Okay, so before we then go over to the TRM paper, let's just summarize here. What matters most from the HRM paper that we should take away before we transition? Yeah, I think that the number one piece to take away is this outer refinement loop. The outer refinement loop scales. And there's a great breakdown. Basically the Sapien authors didn't really do scaling ablations on every single one of the inputs, but this guy Constantine at Francois Chollet's company actually did. It's this amazing breakdown that he posted on YouTube. But basically the main takeaway is that the outer refinement loops are the main beneficiary, the main reason why these things work so well. Alexia basically found this in parallel and scales up and shows that you can get rid of a lot of all this other stuff. Okay, so like a lot of machine learning, the follow-on paper is basically "delete 75% of the first paper" and keep the magic. So okay, what's the magic then? What's the part that actually matters in terms of what stays in the TRM paper? Yeah, so I think if I break it down into two major things: this outer refinement loop thing is really great and works really well. And this truncated backprop through time, where T equals 1 is actually completely sufficient. And that's very counterintuitive. Which is what HRM found. Which is what HRM found, and TRM does a little bit further. Rather than going through just one call to the H-net and the L-net, it actually goes through one full recursion loop. So if I do it 16 times I just go back through one time, and that is kind of sufficient. And if you do it with this pseudo fixed-point iteration thing where you keep hitting it with a gradient at every single step, it weirdly works. It seems like another thing that changed was having this sort of double layer of higher-order thinking and lower-order thinking. It seems like it collapsed it down into just a single one. What's the intuition there? Yeah, so it's interesting. She actually ablates having two separate networks versus just having one. I guess the more important space is the variable scope, that you should have low-level features and high-level features but use the same network. The same network can extract both basically. Yeah, you weight share between the L-net and the H-net and it's just called "net." You do just one transformer layer versus the four like they do in Sapien. She calls it X and Y, which I find very confusing. X, Y, Z, which is very confusing. In the paper, Y is actually like latent space; it's like Z basically. And it is not a label. Right. Which really threw me through a loop. Anyway, we'll go through some code here and I'll walk you through it. I replaced all of her nomenclature and used the Sapien notation. Okay, cool. And now before we dive into the code, recursion now gives you a bunch of advantages over transformers where rather than having 500 or a million transformer layers and tons of parameters, you get compute depth basically without this parameter depth. And the optimization process looks more like an iterative expectation maximization algorithm. So both of them kind of had the same kind of EM-y feeling thing where we update Z L conditioned upon the input X and Z H, the last Z H, and then we keep updating Z L. And then we go holding, we update Z H conditioned upon Z L. And actually it's just Z L, it's not even X. Z L is like your local scoped variables that are being overwritten. And then Z H, as Alexia makes this point, that is a candidate answer, a proposed latent answer that is just an embedding space away, one MLP lookup away from the true answer. So you're kind of like EMing just to zoom out a little bit. You're maximizing the probability of the correct information stored in your memory conditioned on a given output and vice versa in parallel. Yeah, and it's really like—let's actually think of Sudoku. It's actually a really natural way to think about what's actually happening under the hood. Sudoku is an incomplete puzzle. You can't guess every cell at any one time. It's designed where you can only guess one or two cells based on the available information. So it's an incompressible problem. You actually can't do it unless you're just randomly guessing, which is a very high combinatorial space. So what the Z L is doing is some type of "let me try this, try that, do some computation, think about local things," and then it proposes and sends it to Z H. Z H fills it in, and now we have like a little bit more of a filled-in Sudoku puzzle. And the training process is training the algorithm to know to do that, right? It's maximizing that strategy for what you save. Without chain of thought. Without chain of thought, exactly. That's the most important part. It is able to discover things without being teacher-forced. Should we look at some code? Let's do it. So, I mean they're remarkably similar. But basically, you start out with some Z H and Z L that are just zeros. You have some input embedding space, we go from X raw to X, which is the maze state or initial maze state. And then with no grad, you don't pass any gradients back through this. This is the trick basically to not backprop through time. Here are two of the three recursion levels. You have this—they do this just for simplicity—but I hit Z L TL times and then once for modulo TL then I hit the Z H and then I do it again and again. You're updating Z L conditioned upon Z H and X and then you update Z H conditioned upon Z L. Exactly. And then you don't really need this; this is just for cleanliness to show clearly that there's no gradients occurring above this line. Just kind of freezing the weights past that. And then I hit L-net and H-net one more time. Which is the same thing as up above. So it's literally just the no-grad thing running one more time. Exactly. And that's your HRM model. They use 2 and 2 is completely sufficient. If you actually go much higher, Constantine showed very clearly that it doesn't actually help. So that's two of the three recursions you said. The third is in the train loop and at the test loop. They both have this M test or N supervision, which Alexia calls deep supervision. You do this N sup times during training and then during test time there's a different hyperparameter for how many times it recurses, which is M test. They're actually the same. If you actually train on 16 and you test on only 1, you get like almost all the performance. So it's actually quite interesting that this is just redundant. So setting this to 1 is actually like... But presumably for more complicated problems having more test time compute is still useful. Yeah, for sure. So we call our HRM, we get some loss, we backprop through just those two little parts and then we step, we zero out the gradient, but we do not update Z H and Z L. These are still the same init, so that's the really important detail there. And then so we go back, we pass in the Z H and the Z L from the previous one, so now this is actually not the same batch. Because we have updated Z H and Z L, so it's in a different part of the latent space. And then at test time, it's simply the three loops. There's your outer refinement loop which turns out recursion was important during training but test time recursion was actually not that important, which is kind of counterintuitive. And then the HRM inside that has your two other loops. So pretty simple. Now for TRM, the main two changes are that they collapse L-net and H-net into just "net." These are one transformer layer instead of four. Alexia actually shows that going deeper actually didn't help. Yeah, and actually on some tasks, it was just a feed-forward net. On Sudoku, the MLP actually outperformed the attention. So there's the weight sharing. And then instead of going back just the one iteration, you actually backprop through one full latent recursion step. All the way through. Let me just walk through this a little bit. We have the same starting point. We're doing this six times, and then we go one more time here. And then we do our deep recursion, this is the outer loop N sup times. Again we have the no-grad and the detach, and then this is where it's different. I am calling this latent recursion after the detach. So one full recursive loop is happening in the backprop. And so that's the main difference in the optimization. Otherwise it's effectively the same. And then it outputs, and you're good to go and you train it exactly the same way as before. And so in many ways it's sort of a simplification. You're collapsing certain parts of it, you're simplifying this net architecture. It's slightly more complicated along this backprop through time part because you're actually backpropping through more than you did before. Which is actually why she needs to make the model smaller. It's a 28 million parameter model for HRM, and she brings it down to a 7 million parameter model for TRM. It actually gets from 70% to 87% on ARC Prize 1. She makes the model four times smaller, but because it has that recursion, it actually outperforms. There is one researcher named Melanie Mitchell that writes about this very phenomenon. It is sufficient, not necessary, to go bigger and get better performance, and it is sufficient and not necessary to add more recursion. And so where I'm really excited is what happens if you do both. And you're still limited by backprop through time. Even Alexia is limited by backprop that last step from a memory perspective for sure. So if you can make the model really big and you have lots of recursion and we do something else other than backprop through time, then we can get exactly all the benefits of this and all the benefits of the giant LLM. So now to wrap up, what does this mean for the field of AI research? How should people think about where these models fit into the current span of research happening? Well, I think for one, recursion's important and it's not going away. Clearly the benefit is here, and you've seen things like the recursion language models out of Google. The next one is this outer refinement loop, like truncated backprop through time T equals 1. I think that that is a really powerful idea and the fact that that works so well, we have yet to really explore that extremely and really understand what's happening there. And then the third is that idea of, okay, we know that recursion works. We have these tiny recursive models that are 7 million parameters and can solve what a trillion parameter model can't solve. The right answer is to take the amazingness here and take the amazingness here. When you take the benefit of both these TRMs and these giant models and you actually slam them together, I think that it is just going to take off. Yeah, one of the things that's really interesting about these TRMs and HRMs is they're not general-purpose models; they're task-specific. The model trained to do Sudoku cannot do ARC Prize inherently. Versus the LLMs that are general-purpose models. So I think that's where the interesting overlap might come. If you can make these more general-purpose agents that can do more complex reasoning to achieve that, it seems like you can have really efficient architectures to do scalable reasoning. Right. A lot of the view of what these LLMs are doing is finding really amazing embedding representation spaces. But reasoning inside that space is actually not done all that much. Yeah, it's always through the token space. And so what you can imagine is we found some really cool latent space where things are just nicely semantically separated. But now in that space use these tiny reasoning models, use some type of recursion inside that. I think that's going to really work. Francois, thanks so much for breaking it all down for us.

‘Write-Downs To Zero’: The $1.8 Trillion Private Credit Warning - Danielle DiMartino Booth

Kitco NEWS(767.0K)·23:30·6.6K views·2026-05-01
Danielle DiMartino BoothCEO and Chief Strategist, QI Research
TL;DRDespite surface-level market optimism, Danielle DiMartino Booth warns of an imminent "industrial recession" and a looming liquidity crisis in the $1.8 trillion private credit market as corporate margins collapse and actual labor data reveals significant middle-class distress.

TL;DR

Despite surface-level market optimism, Danielle DiMartino Booth warns of an imminent "industrial recession" and a looming liquidity crisis in the $1.8 trillion private credit market as corporate margins collapse and actual labor data reveals significant middle-class distress.

The Federal Reserve’s "Fractured" Leadership

  • Unprecedented Dissent: The recent 8-to-4 vote on policy represents the largest committee split since 1992, signaling a loss of consensus as inflation remains sticky.
  • Transition to Kevin Warsh: Incoming Chair Kevin Warsh (expected mid-May) inherits a "fractured" Fed.
      • Powell's Role: Outgoing Chair Jay Powell has signaled he will step back into a governor role and remain deferential, avoiding becoming a "shadow chair."
      • Alternative Data: Warsh is expected to utilize alternative data sources—such as small business bankruptcies—rather than traditional metrics to justify interest rate cuts.
  • Policy Lag: Booth argues the Fed is already "too late" to the easing process, noting that payroll revisions show net job destruction in the second and third quarters of 2025.

The $1.8 Trillion Private Credit & CRE Warning

  • Write-Downs to Zero: Booth highlights that firms like Ares Capital are already writing down big investments from par to zero, signaling the end of a seven-year period of low due diligence.
  • Liquidity Run Risks: The Financial Stability Board is investigating the 1.8 trillion-dollar private credit market as retail investors follow institutions into these illiquid assets.
      • Redemption Requests: High redemption requests from "skittish" retail investors could force a broader market sell-off if they cannot exit illiquid private credit positions.
  • Commercial Real Estate (CRE) Distress: With 30-year Treasury yields tapping 5%, the relief regional banks expected from falling rates has vanished.
      • Fire Sales: Office properties are seeing 90% discounts and distressed sales are at a decade high, further tightening lending standards.

The K-Shaped Economy and Labor Market Decay

  • The "Severance Cliff": While jobless claims appear low (189,000), Booth notes that only one in four unemployed Americans are actually collecting benefits, with a 40% exhaustion rate.
      • Underemployment: The job market has shifted toward low-quality, temporary, and contractor roles designed to execute AI implementations that eventually replace permanent staff.
  • Consumer Exhaustion: Subprime and near-prime borrowers are now spending 16% of their monthly income on debt payments.
      • Discretionary Slump: Personal savings have hit their lowest point since 2022, leading to record-low numbers of Americans planning summer road trips.
  • Housing Market Freeze: Mortgage rates returning to 6.3% have frozen the market, but re-listings are rising as layoffs (at firms like Meta and Estée Lauder) force families to monetize their primary assets.

Industrial Recession and Global Capital Shifts

  • Manufacturing Margin Squeeze: US manufacturers are "panic buying" inventory to get ahead of higher input costs and energy crises, while simultaneously slashing labor costs to protect margins.
  • The "Industrial Recession": Booth predicts a "cliff dive" in manufacturing this summer as companies struggle with weaker demand and higher carrying costs.
  • Institutional Pivot to Gold: Crypto entities (like Tether) and central banks are stockpiling physical gold, signaling a breakdown in institutional trust in the US dollar amid fears of "socialist" fiscal policies and persistent double-digit inflation.

Notable Quotes

"We've gone from par to zero. So the cracks continue to emerge, the distress continues to become more apparent... there’s big trouble when you’ve had effectively a seven-year period of very low due diligence." — Danielle DiMartino Booth
"The exhaustion rate is 40%. So we're not actually looking at data through a true prism... only one in four Americans who are unemployed are actually collecting benefits." — Danielle DiMartino Booth
"If the Fed is forced to lower interest rates too late because of what Jamie Dimon is referencing—a potential liquidity event—there's just going to be less money for [retirees] to spend." — Danielle DiMartino Booth
"We are seeing 90% discounts on office properties. Office distressed sales are at a decade high." — Danielle DiMartino Booth
Welcome back. I'm Jeremy Szafron and we're looking at some incredibly conflicting data in the markets today. Equities are extending their longest weekly rally since 2024 and jobless claims have dropped to levels we haven't seen since 1969. Yet the structural numbers point to a very different reality. US publicly held debt just crossed 31.265 trillion dollars, officially pushing past 100% of GDP. And we just witnessed an eight-to-four split at the Federal Reserve, the largest committee dissent since 1992, over how to manage sticky inflation. At the corporate level, Jamie Dimon is out with a fresh warning that a credit-led recession could be much worse than anticipated. Of course, to help us read the actual data and understand where capital is moving, we're joined by Fed insider Danielle DiMartino Booth, CEO of QI Research. Danielle, great to have you back on the show. It's great to be back with you, Jeremy. Thank you for having me. Busy, busy week of data. Of course, we had the Fed meeting. Let's start with the Federal Reserve. We just saw that eight-to-four vote on the policy statement, which is the largest committee dissent we saw since 1992 as I mentioned. I mean, you now have Kashkari, Hammack, and Lori Logan explicitly coming out to say it's no longer appropriate to signal cuts. What does a split this severe tell us about their actual control over the current inflation cycle? You know what? It doesn't tell us much. It tells us that A, Kashkari is a well-known, notorious dove, and the signal that it sends is good luck forming a consensus to future chair Kevin Warsh. I think that that was the message that they were trying to send. This is not going to be your grandfather's Fed. You're going to have trouble really corralling the ranks and dissent may become part of your job on day one. I think that that really is the signal that it was sending. There were payroll revisions out the same morning that the Fed met that showed that on average in the third quarter of 2025, 53,000 jobs were lost. And that followed Q2 where also on a monthly average rate jobs were being destroyed on a net basis. So two consecutive quarters in 2025 tells you that not only is it appropriate for the Fed to be in an easing bias, but that they should be easing now and that they're too late to the easing process. And you know, you bring up Warsh. I mean, we got to talk about it. That major leadership shift is approaching. I mean, he's on track to take the helm mid-May. How will a Warsh-led Fed approach this specific bind of, you know, also sticky inflation differently than the outgoing Powell regime? Well, A, I don't think it's an outgoing Powell regime—not yet. But you know, I'm going to take a pause there for a second and say that during the press conference, he was so deferential to incoming chair Warsh that I think he's going to be the least of Warsh's problems. He said he was going to step back to a governor role and play that part as opposed to try and be basically a shadow Fed chair just because of his physical presence there. So I think there's a certain irony there. And I think that Warsh is going to do a good job of listening to some of Jay Powell's work that he's done on services disinflation, which is accelerating to the downside. And I think that Kevin Warsh is also young enough to appreciate alternative measures of employment that somebody might like, oh, I don't know, like me, might point out. We have the lowest jobless claims since 1969, but Warsh might come back and say that's great, but only one in four Americans who are unemployed, more than 7 million, are actually collecting benefits. And the exhaustion rate—in other words, any given state might give you 28 weeks of unemployment benefits—the exhaustion rate is 40%. So we're not actually looking at data through a true prism. And I think that Warsh may take a different approach to using alternative data sources. That actually helps him be able to get in there and start cutting interest rates, citing things like, oh, I don't know, near-record levels of small business bankruptcies. Yeah, yeah. These are things that, I mean, you've been talking about, Danielle, for years since you and I have been chatting on this program. I mean, on that point, because it sounds like you think Powell will kind of step out of the way—I mean, if he stays on as a governor after stepping down as chair, Warsh doesn't inherit a Fed with an internal opposition block from day one then? No, no. In fact, he made a point about five times during that press conference of saying that it is going to be Warsh's Fed and that he was not going to try and be somebody who was getting in the way of that. It was very respectful, it was very deferential, and it showed a lot of integrity on the part of Jay Powell. I don't agree with Jay Powell's policies lately. I think he's been politically holding back on easing because he's been under attack by the Trump administration. But I thought that he showed a lot of integrity to say it's not going to be my Fed anymore, it's going to be Kevin Warsh's. Well said. Okay, well I want to factor in—we'll stay on the Fed just for a second because I want to factor in the bond market—because the 30-year Treasury yield just tapped that 5% mark and major banks like Morgan Stanley are completely scrapping their rate cut forecasts. I mean, if the long end of the curve stays anchored this high, does the regulatory cover for regional banks and their commercial real estate loans finally break? Commercial real estate loans. You just retweeted out an opus that I wrote about commercial real estate. And the real problem here, we have to think back to 2018 when Jay Powell himself encountered a liquidity crisis. And that made all of the macroeconomic data go into the background because he had to address the liquidity crisis. If banks continue with this higher as you say long rate environment—banks have been waiting and waiting and waiting for interest rates to fall to get some kind of relief on these, it's a 5 trillion dollars of commercial real estate loans—and interest rates are going in the opposite direction, then you're going to see more loss realization. You're going to see, as the Wall Street Journal reported last week, you know, we're seeing 90% discounts on office properties. Office distressed sales are at a decade high. The more losses that are realized in a high interest rate environment, the more lending standards are going to tighten and the worse it's going to feed and bleed through into this private credit situation. Yeah, yeah. Actually, that was a good transition. I was going to ask you more on the maturities and kind of that refinancing failures, but I think we should talk about this private credit. I mean, Jamie Dimon yet again—I know every week he almost has a headline here—but he just came out and explicitly warned that a credit-led recession will be worse than what people think, specifically in the private credit segment. Are the cracks—and I rewatched our, I haven't had you on since January, it's been so crazy—but I rewatched that interview and you were talking about these types of cracks. Is that what's finally breaking open here? Oh, I think that's definitely the case. And it's interesting because kind of the financial media in the broadest sense is trying to gloss over a lot of what's happening. But when you see a headline that says Ares Capital writes down to zero, three big investments, you're like wait a minute, zero? We've gone from par to zero. So the cracks continue to emerge, the distress continues to become more apparent. And I happen to agree with Jamie Dimon. I think there's big trouble when you've had effectively a seven-year period of very low due diligence in making loans and levering companies up. They are now blowing up because the assumption has always been in there, you know, if anything goes wrong the Fed's going to go to the zero bound. Well, that ain't happening. And I mean, following up on that, you just talked about it, those credit players, Ares, Blackstone just released what they're calling internal scorecards. They are trying to kind of prove their software loans are insulated from any AI disruption. Are these firms anticipating massive valuation markdowns right now? They are, because that's usually what you see when you're watching a football game and all of a sudden the offensive coordinator gets thrown off the field and the defensive coordinator comes in to play the lead role. And I think that's what we're seeing here. I think we're going to see more large write-downs, I think we're not going to see a cessation in high redemption requests from these funds. And again, the whole point right now of how oil prices and the persistence of high oil prices, right? We've got gasoline in the United States at untenablely high levels, and how this filters through to higher interest rates is a death knell for all of these private credit firms that keep hoping and praying God give us low zero interest rate policy or we're going to have to do major write-downs. That's the juncture that we're at right now and that's what Jamie Dimon is rightly recognizing. Yeah, I mean, you brought up illiquidity as well.
