I mean, we're also just learning this morning the Financial Stability Board is officially examining the risks of retail investors piling into this 1.8 trillion dollar private credit market. Are we watching the early stages of a real liquidity run in that space? I mean, feels like it. I think we are, because the liquidity run was preceded by public pensions and life insurance companies piling into this very illiquid asset class. Now it's being followed by retail investors, but remember retail investors get very skittish or you wouldn't see Blue Owl's stock trading like a penny stock. So they want their money immediately and when the reality of gee, this stuff is not liquid, I better get out of it, that can cause problems for the broader market. Because if they're not able to get the redemptions that they're seeking, then they start to sell off whatever else they have that is liquid. You and I have talked about that K-shaped economy. I mean, I was looking at some of that data this morning. I mean, Wall Street is obviously celebrating because 81% of the S&P 500 just beat their first quarter earnings. But TransUnion just released data showing debt payments are now eating up 16% of monthly income from subprime and near-prime borrowers. I mean, is this corporate earnings boom basically masking a middle-class recession right now? It is, and I think that corporations were saying, you know, let's get past the tariff terror. But that completely has changed. Completely has changed because now they're having to panic buy again, which is killing their margins. I don't think corporate America was planning on having a kind of a redux, a sequel to this margin squeeze, and yet it's right upon them. We saw some very, very interesting data out of the ISM this morning on the manufacturing side where they're lowering their employment. Employment is contracting at a recessionary level among US manufacturers, and yet they're increasing their orders. So they're trying to buy ahead of what they know are going to be higher input costs because of this energy crisis, but at the same time, they're reducing the only cost over which they have control, which is labor. And that tells you that this earnings boom is on—it's on borrowed time. Yeah, yeah. I mean, that next phase of the margin squeeze as you mentioned. So companies—I just want to get that for the audience because companies kind of front-loaded inventory to beat tariffs, but now, I mean, weaker demand, higher carrying costs, less pricing power. Yeah, and that's exactly what you're seeing from the TransUnion data and it's spreading. It would not surprise me in the least to see the current administration say, oh, well we were going to get really tougher on student loans starting on July the first, let's just push that off until after the midterms comes and goes. Because at the bottom of the K, the distress is getting worse and worse. Yeah, yeah. And I mean, we talked about that personal savings rate—it just dipped to its lowest point since 2022. If the consumer is exhausting their savings just to keep up with the basic cost of living, I mean, how much longer can discretionary retail spending hold up? Look, the Conference Board just reported the lowest number on record of Americans planning to take a vacation by car. And that's like road trips, summertime, summer vacations are being cancelled by families. And you know, the other interesting connection to make there is that food at home in the CPI report was 0.0. Zero. I mean, the implication is that people are actually because they're having to spend more to fill up their gas tank that they're spending less at the grocery store. I mean, these are some very distressing signals coming out of households and services disinflation, which Powell watches very closely, is going to become one of what Kevin Warsh talks about a lot. There's an offset—there's only so much money you can squeeze out of a deflationary wage environment, and you're going to see that in services disinflation, which is exactly what the travel industry is communicating to us. That's wild, some of those stats. We got to talk about houses just quick. I mean, US mortgage rates just jumped back to 6.3%, effectively freezing the spring housing market for new buyers. I mean, with affordability this kind of constrained, I mean, are we heading for a structural breakdown in the housing market or is this just kind of a prolonged freeze? Well, I don't think it's a prolonged freeze anymore because we're seeing re-listings go up so much. So there were a lot of sellers who said a year ago, you know what, I'm going to just pull my house off the market, I'm just going to wait for mortgage rates to fall, that's going to benefit me and allow me to get the price that I want for my home. That's not an option right now. And they still, a lot of them still need to sell their home. We know that layoffs—Estee Lauder announced this morning, Meta announced big layoffs, the Chicago Board of—I think that it was either the CME or the CBOE said that it was reducing its workforce by 20%. I mean, in these types of environments, there's only so much you're going to get out of the consumer and the layoffs just keep coming. Yeah, yeah. That leads into the labor market. I mean, we talked about that initial jobless claims just dropped to 189,000. But six weeks or six months ago when you were on this show, you kind of pointed out that laid-off workers were living on severance packages which were kind of skewing the data. Are we about to see that severance cliff hit the employment numbers here? Remember in October we had this massive huge number of layoffs. That was followed again with another huge number in January. And you're right, these people only have those severance payments for so long. You know, the most telling anecdote that I have heard of late is that a friend of mine was leaving a sports tournament with his high schooler and the head coach had a Lyft sticker on his car. I mean, there's something wrong when in 2019 for every entry-level job position there were 100 applicants and today there are 300—300 applicants for every new open job. Underemployment and unemployment among college graduates is going to become very problematic and very political, by the way, headed into these midterms. Right? Gen Z, Gen Z and millennials added together are 52.5% of US voters. Yeah, wild. And you know, I don't know if you saw there was a new report this morning, staffing firms are now saying companies are leaning more on temporary workers and contractors instead of any permanent hires, partly because of that AI and stuff. I mean, does that tell us the labor market's not collapsing, but just really low quality at this point too? It is low quality. And I lay you money that a lot of these temporary workers who are being brought on are being brought on to execute AI such that they don't—A, they don't need the temporary worker anymore, and then they don't need to replace the temporary worker with a permanent worker if the temporary worker has successfully executed AI, reducing the number of jobs that are needed in any given company. Let's get back to—I just want to touch on the structural inflation argument. John Authers recently argued that the retiring boomers and shrinking workforce are going to drive up service inflation regardless of what central banks do. If demographics are taking the wheel here, does the Fed even have the tools left to get us back to 2%? That is a very, very good question. But with deference to John who does wonderful work—I read everything he writes—there's only so much that they're going to be able to spend when healthcare inflation is still not under control. So we're living longer, home prices for baby boomers are falling, they're going to look to monetize the asset that is their biggest asset on their balance sheet just so that they can sustain their living. And if the Fed is forced to lower interest rates too late because of what Jamie Dimon is referencing, a potential liquidity event, and therefore they're making even less on their savings—so they're not making 3% on their savings, they're making 2% on their savings—there's just going to be less money for them to spend. They're going to want to go take a cruise, but will they be able to? Yeah, yeah. And I mean looking globally, I saw Brent today on some new reports, it's pulled back from its recent highs, which also seems partly tied to the Bank of Japan likely spending 34.5 billion dollars in a single intervention to prop up the Yen. I'm bringing it up because I just want you to kind of explain: how does this foreign intervention change the flow of global capital? Well look, there are energy exporters on a net basis and energy importers on a net basis. And again, there are estimates that suggest that there's been upwards of two trillion—excuse me, yeah, I think trillion dollars of infrastructure damage in the Middle East. It's going to take a long time to build this back up. So there are going to be countries that are constantly trying to make life affordable if they're net energy importers. So it will change global capital flows, it will change what happens at US Treasury auctions as well. I want to get to gold for our Kitco audience obviously. I mean, it's been impressive to watch. I mean, I saw the headlines this morning, Tether's expanding its buying spree right now—they're holding almost 20 billion dollars in bullion. When crypto giants are kind of stockpiling physical gold alongside the central banks here, I mean, what is that signaling about institutional trust in the US dollar right now? Well, again, I'm going to go back to politics even though I'm not trying to be political. But if underemployment with young Americans continues to rise, if unemployment among college graduates continues to rise, and there is a massive blue wave and the idea that the United States is going to take one step closer to being a socialist type of economy—meaning double-digit inflation as far as the eye can see, determined at the polls—then the dollar is going to be under increased attack because it costs a lot of money to implement those kinds of universal basic income types of policies. We saw that right after the pandemic came and it was, oh I don't know, 15 trillion dollars that they had to spend to pay people to not work. If that's the direction that we're going in politically, then yes, the dollar is going to be under increased threat. Meaning gold is going to be more important. Yeah, I mean it certainly has been, just watching it sitting here. I'm wearing the right color today. Yeah, yeah, exactly. You are, you are. Okay, well I'll let you go, but before we do that, I mean, you mentioned the midterms—let's even before that. As we head into these summer months, I mean, what is kind of a biggest single kind of data point that you're tracking right now that you feel the market is ignoring? So what I'm tracking right now is the panic buying ahead of what is expected to be higher input costs while manufacturers are lowering their hiring. I think we're going to see a cliff dive in manufacturing and a realization this summer that we've gone right back into an industrial recession. All right, always a reality check. Danielle DiMartino Booth, CEO of QI Research. And of course, our viewers should also check out your newsletter, The Daily Feather. I was on it this morning going through some of this to prep. And again, our January interview—I mean, you've been talking about this. So I appreciate your time. And you, thank you for having me as always, but let's not wait another this many months to come back and visit again. We'll get you back on soon here. Honestly, the viewers demand it. So appreciate it, Danielle. Thanks for making the time. Take care. All right, that was Danielle DiMartino Booth. We have lots of conflicting data to digest from record low jobless claims to the 31 trillion in national debt, a split Fed, cracks in private data. You have to look past the headlines to understand the full picture. So if you appreciate getting the actual numbers, do me a favor, hit that subscribe button right now. Leave a comment below with the real inflation rate you're experiencing in your city. I do read them all and it helps us shape these interviews. I'm Jeremy Szafron. Thanks for watching.

Can America trust AI? David Sacks makes the case.

POLITICO(211.0K)·41:40·383 views·2026-05-01
David SacksSpecial Advisor to the President
TL;DRDavid Sacks, special advisor to President Trump on AI, argues for a "let them cook" regulatory philosophy of permissionless innovation to ensure American dominance in a global race against China, emphasizing that AI will ultimately solve the very problems it creates.

TL;DR

David Sacks, special advisor to President Trump on AI, argues for a "let them cook" regulatory philosophy of permissionless innovation to ensure American dominance in a global race against China, emphasizing that AI will ultimately solve the very problems it creates.

The Trump Administration’s "Permissionless Innovation" Framework

  • Global Competition: Sacks views AI as an "infinite game" where the U.S. cannot afford to slow down; unilateral disarmament would simply cede leadership to China, where half of the world’s AI developers are located.
  • The Three Pillars of AI Policy:
      • Pro-Innovation: Adopting a "let them cook" philosophy that favors private sector leadership with minimally burdensome federal regulation.
      • Pro-Infrastructure: Aligning AI needs with "drill, baby, drill" energy policies to meet the massive power demands of data centers.
      • Pro-Export: Ensuring American chips and models maintain global market share to preserve U.S. "soft power."
  • Preempting State Regulation: The White House framework aims to provide a national alternative to the 1,200+ bills currently moving through state legislatures, which Sacks characterizes as "knee-jerk regulation."

Targeted Regulation and Public Safety

  • Parental Empowerment: Regarding online child safety and AI chatbots, Sacks argues the "North Star" should be giving parents the tools to decide app usage and screen time rather than government bans.
  • Ratepayer Protection: To combat local opposition to data centers (NIMBYism), the administration secured a pledge from major AI companies to ensure new data centers do not increase residential electricity prices.
      • The "Quid Pro Quo": AI companies are encouraged to become "power companies," bringing their own energy sources in exchange for streamlined building permits.
  • Liability Standards: Sacks opposes holding AI developers liable for the actions of end-users, comparing it to holding Microsoft Excel responsible for a money launderer’s spreadsheets. He supports "red teaming" to prevent models from providing instructions for bioweapons or nuclear devices.

National Security and the "AI Equilibrium"

  • The Pentagon Chain of Command: Sacks criticizes AI companies (specifically citing the Anthropic dispute) that attempt to veto the "lawful uses" of their products by the military. He argues that companies selling to the Department of Defense must respect the established chain of command.
  • Cybersecurity Arms Race: Regarding "Mythos," a potent hacking tool held back from public release, Sacks believes the market will reach a new equilibrium.
      • Defensive Parity: He argues that AI's ability to find coding vulnerabilities will be used by "white hats" to patch systems before "black hats" can exploit them.
  • Orwellian vs. Terminator Risks: Sacks is less concerned about "The Terminator" and more concerned with an "Orwellian" future where government and corporate power merge to surveil and censor citizens.

Economic Outlook and the "Pessimism Gap"

  • Debunking Job Loss Narratives: Sacks claims there is "no discernible disruption" to the labor market yet, citing that less than 5% of recent layoffs are attributable to AI.
      • The Construction Boom: He highlights a massive $650 billion investment in CAPEX, which has led to a 25-30% wage increase for blue-collar workers (electricians, plumbers, and drywallers) building AI infrastructure.
  • White-Collar Transformation: While acknowledging shifts in coding and research, Sacks views AI as a "productivity explosion" and a leveler for young workers, rather than a tool for total job evaporation.
  • The Optimism Deficit: Sacks identifies public fear as the biggest threat to U.S. leadership. He notes that while 83% of Chinese citizens are "AI-optimistic," only 39% of Americans share that view.
AI is going to solve the problem that AI creates. That's kind of my view on where that market is going.

— David Sacks

If in five years we look around the world and all the data centers are running on Huawei chips and DeepSeek models, that means that we lost. We don't want to have that future.

— David Sacks

I just don't think it was a very realistic position for them [Anthropic] to take to say that they can dictate to a government agency what lawful uses of their product should be allowed.

— David Sacks

AI is going to solve the problem that AI creates. That's kind of my view on where that market is going. Hello, hello and welcome to The Conversation. I'm Dasha Burns and on this show, I talk to the most compelling and sometimes unexpected power players in Washington and beyond. And this week, I sat down with David Sacks, special advisor to President Trump on all things AI. In the tech world, there's new concerns over the safety of artificial intelligence. As more companies integrate artificial intelligence into their business models, a new analysis is diving deep into just how much the technology could impact the workforce. Lawmakers are getting insights from industry leaders about security issues presented by today's rapidly advancing technology. What happens when you combine artificial intelligence with deadly weapons? As we see the rapid expansion and adoption of artificial intelligence, Sacks has played a key role in shaping policy around the burgeoning technology. He's a venture capitalist and a Silicon Valley veteran who previously held the title of White House AI and crypto czar. So, when it comes to regulating AI, Sacks approaches the issue with a quote "let them cook" philosophy, meaning he thinks the way for the United States to win the global AI race is to move fast with minimally disruptive regulation. But with that approach comes a lot of questions about disruption to the workforce, lawsuits around problems AI has created, increased energy grid demands, all set to the backdrop of public fears around AI. So, we had plenty to discuss. David Sacks joins the conversation. David Sacks, thank you so much for joining the conversation. Good to be here, thank you. I'm so excited to have you on the show because AI is consuming my brain, I think a lot of people's brains, it's a huge priority for the White House and you have been the guy that has been helping build out the framework, set the agenda of how the US should really handle this. I want to start with that White House AI regulatory framework. So it has these legislative recommendations guided by a vision of quote "permissionless innovation" and quote "minimally burdensome regulation". Now, I've heard your spiel on this, but for some of those that might not be as familiar, I do want you to sort of set out why you think that a technology this powerful, this disruptive, should be left mostly in the hands of private companies to control. Why the "let them cook" philosophy, as you call it? Well, I think the first thing to recognize is that we are in a globally competitive environment. We're not the only country that has advanced AI labs. And as the president declared in a major policy speech he gave on AI in July last year, we have to win this AI race. And I'm not sure it has a definite finish line; some people have said it's more of an infinite game. Either way though, we don't want to fall behind our global competitors because that will have a huge impact on our national security and on our economy. So I think that's the first thing to recognize is that if somehow we slow down or stop AI development, it doesn't mean that AI progress is going to stop, it just means it's going to happen in other countries and specifically China. Something like half the world's AI developers are in China or are Chinese. They have the technological expertise, capabilities, and a lot of the talent. So again, it is just globally competitive and it's not like we can just unilaterally disarm or stop AI development. So I think that's sort of the place to start. And then in that important AI speech, the president laid out a few of his pillars on how we win this AI race. And one of them is being pro-innovation. Doesn't mean we don't have to have any regulations, but generally speaking, we should be pro-innovation. In the United States, the innovation comes from the private sector. Ultimately, it's our companies that push the frontier forward. And so when I say "let them cook", I mean that's really what I'm talking about is trying to have a generally encouraging attitude towards innovation. So that would be pillar number one. And then the president also laid out a pro-infrastructure pillar, which really means being pro-energy, which is something that he's talked about for a decade. That's the "drill, baby, drill", yeah. Right, exactly. And so I think he anticipated the need for energy. Energy is the basis not just for data centers but for our entire economy. Fortunately, he's been pushing for this pro-energy policy for a long time and it's never been more relevant or necessary than now. And then I think the third pillar that he talked about was being pro-export. We want the United States to win this race. The way that you measure winning, I think, in a globally competitive market is based on market share. If in five years we look around the world and all the data centers are running on Huawei chips and DeepSeek models, that means that we lost. We don't want to have that future. What we want to see is that the whole world is running on American chips and American models. That would lead to the best economic results for the United States, it would also lead to the United States having more soft power in this area. So that was another important pillar. And I think it's just important that there are smart things we can do with respect to exports. Obviously we don't want to ship our, you know, leading-edge semiconductors to China or something like that. But generally speaking, we want to help encourage our companies to have the greatest market share. So those were sort of the big pillars of the AI framework. And then let me pause there and then I can tell you how we got to the regulatory framework that we just introduced a few weeks ago. Maybe as part of that you can answer my next question, which was, you mentioned that you're not saying that there shouldn't be any regulation, so I'm wondering where you do see the government's role in regulating this tech and what baseline safety requirements should AI systems meet? Well, what's basically happened over the last year is that a lot of the action moved to the state level. And so state legislatures have been taking up the issue. In fact, there's something like 1,200 bills going through state legislatures right now. Our feeling on that is we don't need that many bills. I mean, a lot of this regulation is sort of knee-jerk regulation, although there are some areas where the states have regulated where we think it's a good idea, or could be a good idea. And so what we did is we looked at all of those and we identified a number of principles that we then included in our framework. So one of them is around online child safety; that's a really salient issue. I think mostly it came from the whole social media debate, but it's also relevant for AI as well with, you know, AI chatbots. So we've said this is a legitimate area for states to regulate. Our north star on this issue is parental empowerment. I mean, ultimately we think it's up to parents to decide what apps their kids should use and for how long, you know, screen time limits and things like that. And so, you know, we would let parents decide whether their kids use AI chatbots and what apps they install and how long they get to use them for. I think, you know, child safety is one area. There's another around communities. You know, with respect to data centers, which is another issue that's become very salient for people, we don't want consumer ratepayers to have to pay for higher electricity prices because of AI data centers. We understand that that would not be popular at all and there's a lot of concerns about that. We've supported this idea of a ratepayer protection pledge, which we got all the major AI companies and hyperscalers to sign up for, in which they agree that for any new data centers that they build, it will not increase residential electricity prices. The quid pro quo for that is making it easier for AI companies to build these data centers if they bring their own power. So that's sort of what the president has talked about is we want our AI companies to become power companies. We think that's a much better way of addressing voter concerns than just having a complete ban on data centers the way that Bernie Sanders is pushing. That would just stop the progress altogether. So that's another area we've talked about in our framework. There's also some pillars around creators, making sure they're protected, and there's some pillars around innovation. Again, we tried to go through all the different areas that the states were regulating and put together our list of principles. There's a lot there. There's so many bills and there's also, I mean, you are seeing this, I don't know if it's a battle but like this process getting navigated both by state legislatures at the national level but also in the courts. There's been this growing wave of AI liability lawsuits across the country. You know, Florida is suing OpenAI after a gunman consulted ChatGPT before carrying out a mass shooting. Other courts have alleged that interactions with ChatGPT result in severe mental health issues, delusions, suicide, and that chat has played a role in encouraging people to do horrible things. Should OpenAI or others be held responsible in any of those cases? Well, I guess it sort of depends. First of all, what's the causation and then what's the liability? I don't necessarily want to judge any of those cases too specifically because I haven't gotten into the fact patterns. And I'm not asking you to play lawyer here, but you've seen these trends, sort of the darker side of AI, similar to what we saw with social media, but obviously AI is a whole other beast. So where do you see that part of online safety? Well, I think people are very concerned about kid safety online and that's a concern that we share. And again, this is where we think that the north star should be parental empowerment. Let the parents decide what their kids use. I should say that there's something like over a billion people who are using AI successfully every day now. It's one of the most quickly adopted product releases in history. I guess we're now about four years into this roughly since the launch of ChatGPT. And so I do think that there are these edge cases, there are these horror stories. I mean, I think that does always happen with a technology that's so rapidly adopted by so many people that there are going to be these anecdotes. I don't think that they've been super common, but yeah, they're out there and I do think that the AI companies have already learned from some of these things. There are these cases of teenagers who had histories of mental health challenges who after using an AI chatbot may have, you know, engaged in self-harm. And I think the AI companies have learned from that and now they're doing a lot more where if they detect that that might be the fact pattern that they will do things like obviously not be encouraging towards that behavior, but maybe show numbers for a suicide prevention hotline and things like that. But like basic things, like should AI companies prevent people from being able to use ChatGPT or these chatbots to like commit a crime or learn how to make a bomb or, you know, things like that? I mean, if they know, they probably should stop it. But the problem is just how do you know? I think it's very hard to know exactly all the ways that their product is being used. And I think that is one of the problems with some of these state liability laws that make the developer liable for the actions of the end user. I just don't think that the developer is in a position to know exactly how their product is being used in the same way that Gmail, for example, doesn't necessarily know whether, you know, its email service is being used in the commission of a crime. I think it would be a very hard thing for Google to figure out is just how is email being used. Or let's say that a criminal, you know, let's say a money launderer uses Microsoft Excel; I mean they use a spreadsheet and it could be a cloud-based one to help perpetrate their crime. It's just very hard to know exactly how the end user is using these products. I mean this is why they have terms of use and they don't want their users using them for unlawful purposes. But you don't think that the developers or these AI companies should be held liable in most of these cases that we've talked about? Well, I don't know, you have to tell me exactly what cases we're talking about. But what I would say is that, I mean, should AI models give you step-by-step instructions on how to create a nuclear bomb or a bioweapon? No. I mean, obviously not. We don't want them doing that. But that's something that's been identified as a potential misuse very, very early on. I mean, going back to even before 2023, and that's why all these major AI labs have red teaming and they test for that kind of stuff to make sure. So, you know, I would say for the obvious misuse, I do think that they should do everything they can to prevent that, obviously. Are they going to be successful at preventing users from being able to use their tools in ways that we don't like? Probably never going to be 100%. I know you probably get this question a lot, so don't roll your eyes at me, but I am curious. You know, Elon Musk, close friend of yours, he's one of the biggest AI entrepreneurs in the industry right now. He's famously not a fan of government bureaucracy or intervention, but even he has called AI the biggest threat to the survival of the human race, right? He's been a supporter of government regulation, including independent safety boards and industry-developed standards. Do you disagree with him on that? Well, a little bit. I mean, I think Elon is maybe a little bit more pessimistic than I am on these topics. I'd say definitely. I mean, that's what's so fascinating as you guys, I think you align on so many stuff, but it does seem like you've come to different conclusions on this one. Well, I don't know. I mean, I think he is basically in a place where he acknowledges that AI is going to be developed regardless. And so what we want to do is remain in control of it. I mean, I don't think we want to hamstring US development to such a point where again, then China just wins the AI race. So I think he understands that the technology is moving forward and we should develop it in a way that allows us to remain in control. So an area, for example, that I agree with him on is we want the AI to be as truthful as possible. And he's pointed out that if you teach the AI to lie, like let's say, you know, you teach it to be woke or whatever, that's very dangerous because then the AI can lie to us about what it's doing. That's not what we want to inculcate in AI. I very much agree with that. By the way, it's not like I don't think there's any potential dangers or risks of AI. I mean, I do acknowledge that there are potential dystopian futures we don't want. In my view, the most likely dystopian future would be something described by George Orwell in 1984 as opposed to James Cameron in The Terminator. My view of it is that the biggest danger is that the government will ultimately use AI to surveil us, control us, censor us; like that kind of thing. And to me, that should be the thing that we should be most afraid of is a marriage of government and corporate power around AI. By the way, that is the path that I think we were on before President Trump's election. If you go back to the Biden executive order on AI, there were really two major things that it did. One was it said that DEI values, which is to say all the woke stuff, should be promoted in AI models. And that's how you ended up with, you know, that Black George Washington controversy around that first release of the Google Gemini model. Well on the flip side, there's been this controversy between Anthropic and the Department of Defense, Department of War. For those who haven't been following, the administration waged a pretty aggressive campaign against the AI company after it refused to let the military use its AI models without ethical guardrails. The administration then ordered federal agencies to stop using its product and declared it a risk to the supply chain. Then President Trump just very recently said that a new deal with Anthropic is possible. First of all, there's a lot there. Is the feud with Anthropic over? Are all agencies going to be free to keep using the company's products in perpetuity? And what do you make of that government versus AI narrative? I think that if you're an AI company and you don't want your product to be used in war, then don't sell to the Department of War. I mean, that's kind of in the name, you know? But they did make a decision that they wanted to be used by the Department of War, they do want to be used throughout the government, they made that decision. And then they tried to insert themselves in the chain of command by having a veto over the lawful uses of their product. By the way, just let me back up and just say that I'm not involved in government procurement, right? So I'm a policy advisor; I was not involved in the Anthropic dispute with the Pentagon, I'm still not involved in it. But just if you want my opinion, I just don't think it was a very realistic position for them to take to say that they can dictate to a government agency what lawful uses of their product should be allowed. I just think you have to make a decision about whether you're going to sell to that government agency or or not. I do think they did try to set themselves up in a way of being superior to the chain of command. I think that's a tough position to take with respect to any government department, but particularly with respect to the Pentagon, I don't think it was particularly feasible. Now, if we get into their concerns for a second, one of their concerns was around mass surveillance. First of all, the Pentagon says that it does not engage in that and that's not a lawful use. What Anthropic I think said is, well there's all these loopholes in the law. If they had come to me as a policy matter and said, hey, we have all these concerns about loopholes in the law, we want to ensure greater privacy for Americans, I would have been very interested in having that conversation. You know, I'm a civil libertarian; I'm very interested in privacy. I mean, when you talk about the Orwellian future, right? Like that's kind of exactly what the concern is. So you think the concern was valid, but they didn't like execute on that concern properly, or what? Well, yeah, I think if you have a concern about there being loopholes in the law, then let's try and change the law. You don't try and do it through a terms of use negotiation with the Pentagon; that's not going to work. I mean, in other words, they're trying to strong-arm the government into agreeing to their changes to the law in exchange for their product. I mean, that's effectively what they're saying they want to do. But look, if they had talked to me about that issue, and by the way, they never did. I did talk to Dario a number of times and we did discuss various issues, but he never raised that concern with me. I think it only came up in the context of this Pentagon negotiation, which is kind of curious. But like I said, if they had been interested in discussing the surveillance issue, I would have been very interested in that conversation. I'm still interested in the conversation. Look, if they want to identify areas of the law that are problematic and could lead to the government using AI to infringe on the privacy of Americans, I would be interested in having that conversation. Do you think that the government though going after an AI company when you talk about AI dominance and the AI race, like does that hurt American AI dominance when the government is in a fight with one of the major companies fueling the AI race? Well, I don't want to comment on their lawsuit specifically. But I think it happens all the time that, you know, you can have companies work with the government, one department of the government, while another department is suing the government. I mean, I think these things happen all the time. Let's talk about Mythos, another part of Anthropic's work. They recently announced Mythos as this new cybersecurity tool, which it says has unprecedented hacking capabilities and is too dangerous to release publicly. How big of a national security threat is this? I think ultimately if everyone does what they're supposed to do, it won't be a national security threat. But here's basically what's going on, is we have this area of cybersecurity where you've got, you know, offense and defense, you've got the white hats versus the black hats, and basically it's all about preventing hackers from breaching systems, right? They try to break in, they try to steal data, maybe plant back doors, do other nefarious things. And you have this arms race between again, cyber offense and cyber defense. Now, AI is going to be a major part of cybersecurity moving forward. Meaning that you're going to have hackers who are powered up by AI models, but then you've got cyber defenders who are powered up by AI capabilities as well. I think that the cybersecurity market will eventually reach a new equilibrium in which both offense and defense have AI capabilities and therefore the danger won't be ratcheted up to a point where we can't control it, right? So public access to Mythos, you don't think is as big of a national security concern? Well no, no, no, no, no, listen, I think holding it back was the right decision because again, because where I'm going with, because I would not want the hackers to have access to it before the defenders, right? What I'm saying is that I believe that the market will eventually reach a new equilibrium in which there's AI capabilities on the part of both hackers and then also cyber defense. And you know, again, AI is going to solve the problem that AI creates. I mean, that's kind of my view on where that market is going. I was going to say, that sounds like the sort of tagline for your AI theory, that AI will solve the problems AI creates. Well, it may not solve every problem. But I do think in the case of hacking, it will. And look, I think that Mythos is a good example. By the way, I think there were multiple reasons why Anthropic held back that model. I mean, supposedly it's like a 10 trillion parameter model, which just means that it's very, very big, it's very expensive. I'm not sure they had the compute to serve that commercially. So I don't know that a commercial release was really even feasible for them. But in any event, they're giving limited access or a preview to, you know, a couple of dozen companies and the government. And what the people who are in this Project Glasswing will do is they're going to use the capabilities to scan their code bases, look for vulnerabilities, and then patch them. So they're going to harden their code bases. Ultimately, Mythos isn't the only AI model that will do this. I mean, all the major AI companies are working on this now. It's really an extension of coding capabilities. So what happened first is that the AI models got really good at coding, and then as a result of that they got good at finding bugs, and then some of those bugs are vulnerabilities, and some of those vulnerabilities can be strung together into an exploit. So what you want to do is use these new AI coding models to find the bugs and the vulnerabilities and patch them before a hacker does the same thing. And I think the net result of it is that we're going to find a lot of dormant vulnerabilities that were there in the software, maybe for a long time, that no one had found before. As long as everyone does what they're supposed to do, which is the CISOs and the IT departments use these new tools to patch the bugs, then actually it will harden cybersecurity around our core systems because those vulnerabilities won't be there anymore. So to me, the "as long as everyone does what you're supposed to do" is like the red flag clause there because Anthropic decided to go and give the government a heads up and to, you know, to hold this back for multiple reasons potentially. You said it yourself, Mythos is not going to be the last AI model that can do something like this or that can be wielded as a massive cyber weapon against national security or financial data. And you know, future companies or other companies that exist now that make something like this might not necessarily handle it in this same way. Like, don't you think that's a ton of power to be in the hands of private profit-minded companies? Like there's no law that says like "Anthropic, you gotta go talk to the government about this before releasing it." I think that the community is generally aware of that, that these AI coding models are getting better and better and they have this trajectory; they're heading in this direction. So I think cybersecurity is an area where everyone in that community knew that AI was going to have a gigantic impact. I think Mythos, like I said, I think maybe it was too big a model to be commercially viable, but it was very valuable as a proof of concept of what an AI-powered cyber model could do. And so, look, I think it has served a useful purpose in terms of creating popular awareness that companies that are in possession of large code bases and the government potentially are going to need to, over the next say six months, harden their systems, use these capabilities to scan their code, find their vulnerabilities and fix them before a bad guy does the same thing. Doesn't that freak you out a little bit? Well, I think if everyone's asleep at the wheel then it would freak me out, but I think people are pretty alert at this point. You gotta have a lot of faith in these CEOs to not be totally freaked here, I think. Well, or their CISOs. I mean, the nightmare of every Chief Information Security Officer is that their system gets hacked. I mean, that's why they get fired. So I do think that they have a fair amount of urgency and they've been clued into this and I've gotten calls now and outreach from private companies that are trying to get into the Glasswing pilot. They're trying to get access to Mythos, and there's just not enough capacity to give it to everyone who wants it. I do think that Mythos won't be the last model that has cyber capabilities. I mean, all the models that are good at coding will be good at cyber. And so it's just a matter, I think, of, you know, making sure that the people who are playing defense get ahead of this. And I do think it is smart to not just make these capabilities publicly available before again the defense has had a chance to incorporate the lessons. I want to talk about the politics of AI for a minute because, you know, you talked about Orwell, you talked about James Cameron; they're different visions of this, but both of them make people pretty nervous. Poll after poll here in the US shows that a lot of people feel concerned, more concerned than excited about AI. I was looking at a poll that said nearly 75% of Americans think the government isn't doing enough to regulate it and a majority of Americans oppose building AI data centers in their communities. Is the administration out of touch with Americans on this issue? I don't think so. I mean, I think we already know where these things are at. I mean, let's just take the data centers as an example. So we know that there is a strong NIMBY backlash right now to the data centers and one of the big reasons for that is that people are afraid that these data centers are going to make their electricity prices go up. And it's true that if a data center plugs into the grid in a local community without adding power and just is a draw on power, that could make rates go up, absolutely. There's no reason for a community to want that data center if it's going to make their electricity prices go up. The question is how you react to that, right? I mean, I think we all see that unfettered, unrestricted data centers with no conditions is not popular. One reaction to it is just to ban the data centers. I mean, that's what Bernie Sanders wants to do. I think the President's approach is a lot better, which is the obvious one, which is to say, look, if you want to build a data center, you have to bring your own power. And in fact, that not only protects ratepayers against price increases, but it could bring their prices down because when the data centers build their own power, they can sell back to the grid when they're not at peak usage and also they have an incentive to pay for grid upgrades. So, you know, with scale, the more scale you have in general, the more that brings prices down. I think that is just one piece of the concern though. I mean overall Americans don't really trust AI. Like, what can and should companies and governments do to build that trust? Well, look, I agree with you. I mean, one of the things I've said, you know, one of the things the President said is that America's ahead of China by a lot. I say that America's ahead in every category except one, which is optimism. Stanford did a study internationally of different populations' views of AI and they asked people "do you think this will be more beneficial than harmful?" China was something like 83% of the population was AI-optimistic. Oh we're, we're way below that, right? Yeah, I think we're at like 39%. So we're under 40 and they're over 80. And that impacts this race that you're talking about, right? Public perception matters. Totally. No, I think it's probably the biggest threat to our winning the AI race, or at least our leadership in it, which is that we might, out of fear or the public's mistrust of AI, do something that kind of shoots ourselves in the foot. And by the way, I'm not saying that we shouldn't do anything. I mean, again, I think we should have targeted solutions to the problems that are raised, like we just talked about with the data centers, like we talked about with online child safety and self-harm, and like we talked about with cybersecurity. I just think that we should have targeted solutions to specific harms that are raised as opposed to, you know, operating in this environment of sort of vague fear. Because it's not really clear exactly what it is that the people who are really afraid want, other than for everything just to stop. You know, that's like King Canute ordering the tides to stop, right? The tides are not going to stop; China's not going to stop. So I just think we have to be realistic here and I just think that again, as we identify specific harms, we should have specific solutions. There's also been, you know, these cases of violence around AI. You know, a man was charged with attempted murder for an attack against the home of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman. He allegedly carried an anti-AI manifesto and a list of other AI executives to target. I mean, first of all, what does that say about the state of things right now and what obligation does a government have in responding to growing divisions and even violence around AI? Well, I mean, anytime there's violence like that, it's totally unacceptable. And I do think it is a foreseeable consequence of some of the overheated rhetoric around AI. I don't think that AI poses all the safety issues it's been accused of and I think it's, you know, beyond wrong to try and take that out on a corporate executive. It's an example of how the rhetoric on this is out of control. I don't blame the media exclusively for this, but I do think that the media is way too focused on the negatives of this technology as opposed to the positives. There's a lot of positives right now for the economy, for economic growth, for productivity, for medical treatments and cures, and it doesn't get the coverage that, say, the coverage around job fears gets. By the way, there's also, let's just talk about the job loss fears for a second. Yeah, we kind of can't not talk about the job part of this, because for everyday Americans, like, that's a huge concern. It is a huge concern, but is there data to support it? And I would argue, no. Now, we can debate what's going to happen in the future, but I don't think the media accurately presents the current state of things. There's a study by Yale, the Yale Budget Lab, which concluded no discernible disruption to the labor market in the first three years after the launch of ChatGPT. So far they have not found a disruption to the labor market. You look at things like the Challenger Grey report, which tallies up the number of layoffs in the economy; less than 5% of the layoffs that were announced last year were attributable to AI. This is a relatively small number. You know, we have over 100 million people working in the American economy, I think something like 150 million, and only 50,000 layoffs last year were attributable to AI. It just was not a big number. And meanwhile, we never talk about the job gains. So we are seeing a construction boom right now that is leading to a boom in blue-collar jobs in the construction industry. You have $650 billion being spent on CAPEX just this year. That's a 2% tailwind to GDP. You're seeing the wages of jobs like electricians, plumbers, the workers who hang drywall or pour concrete or install equipment, build roads; their wages are up 25 to 30%. They can't even hire enough of them. We have a shortage of blue-collar workers. Well, that's been the case for a long time, right? Like my theory, and, you know, you can correct me if I'm wrong here, it is just a theory, but is that like it is going to be white-collar jobs that are more impacted by AI than blue-collar. Like I think all the time about, you know, the junior staff in newsrooms for example, or in Senate offices or wherever, putting together a research packet, like writing a basic breaking news article, doing some of that like intellectual labor but really baseline intellectual labor where pretty soon, maybe even right now, like ChatGPT or Claude or any of these chatbots could do as good or even a better job than like a kid right out of college. But like the way that kid right out of college becomes a senior person at a company is by doing that grunt work first, and then, you know, it's that pipeline that I think is going to get really disrupted by AI. How do you protect that, when, you know, companies are going to look at this as a, you know, why would I spend money to hire a bunch of junior kids when I can have AI do this for me? Well, I think AI is good at automating some tasks, but it's very hard to automate an entire job away. It's hard to automate away a purpose. With respect to these young hires, a lot of them know how to use AI very well. They're AI natives, which you could argue makes them more productive and it's a leveler because they're able to contribute more quickly in the workplace. That is a question we should be asking a lot is just given the challenge of the technology, what do you do about it? My view is that you have to lean in; you want our teenagers for example to be using these tools. It's not something that we should be acting like we want to protect them from. Yes, there are specific harms to protect them from, but in general, I mean I would hate for us to treat AI the way that we sometimes talk about social media, which is it's just harmful and we have to try and get our teenagers to spend as little time on it as possible. I just think it's a very different medium and I think we want our, you know, next generation of workers to be AI natives. I think that's going to make them more productive. Now, with respect to your point about white-collar workers, so I agree with you; that's where the impact is going to be. But let's just say, I think when people start making these sweeping generalizations about massive job loss, I think they're just oversimplifying what's going on. Let me give you an example: so coders. The breakout application of AI models right now beyond just the chatbots has been in coding. This is the thing that's really driving the usage forward right now is that the AI models have gotten extremely good at coding. Anthropic's revenue is exploding because of this. I've even tried vibe coding, David; it's been very exciting for me. Exactly. So you would think, okay, well if AI models are really good at coding, we won't need coders anymore. But if you look at the demand for software engineers, the last study I saw showed that the number of job recs was up 10% year over year. You think that's gonna last though or is that just lagging behind a little bit? Well, you still need someone to prompt the model; you still need someone to supervise. But what if like a Dasha Burns can prompt, and you know, I have zero understanding of coding and I can make an app on my own? I think a lot of small businesses for example would have hired a coder or an engineer to develop something for them and now they can do it on their own, which on the one hand is great for the small business, but on the other hand is not great for the other small business or employee that would have otherwise had a job. Well, I think you're right that the coding capabilities allow companies that previously weren't able to hire software developers to now generate code, which I think will just unleash a productivity explosion for the economy because we can just use software a lot more. But I don't think that's going to put software developers out of work. For one thing, there was a huge latent demand for software developers. I mean, I'm based in Silicon Valley and as long as I can remember, we haven't been able to hire enough software developers for all the companies, all the startups that are operating here. And this has historically been the most attractive place for software developers to go. So if Silicon Valley can't hire enough software developers, that's even more true in the rest of the economy. So look, I think what's happening is that for the first time, large swaths of the economy actually get access to software developers and access to the ability to generate code and to create their own bespoke applications. Now, you asked "will this put coders out of business completely?" I think the answer to that is no. You still need someone to look under the hood. If there's a problem, just because you drive a car doesn't mean you necessarily know how to fix it if something breaks, and you still need someone who knows how to do that. Moreover, if you try to take your bespoke application that you've vibe coded and it's, you know, maybe it's running on your computer and you try to turn that into a production-grade service that you offer on the internet, you're gonna start running into issues. They're not necessarily insurmountable, but you're going to need a coder or you're going to need someone who's very technologically sophisticated to help you solve those problems. So, again, I think this is why we're not seeing the total evaporation of coding jobs; they're shifting their activities from writing code—they're writing less code—but they're doing more, you could say, management of the new AI agents. They're prompting the models more, and they're supervising more. So David, I think it's safe to say given the data around American pessimism versus optimism on AI, that like most average people are not quite where you are with the excitement about what AI can do. Given that, I mean, do you think there could be a downside to how much this administration has leaned into AI when it comes to midterms? Could the fears that people have harm Republicans in 2026 and potentially in 2028 if the White House and this administration are going so hard into this? Well, we have put forward a national AI regulatory framework. You're right that if you poll people and say "do you want to see AI more regulated or less", they will say more. But if you ask them "do you want the federal government to do it or your state", they will actually say the federal government. So we have put forward a national framework; we don't think it should be excessively onerous. We think it should be targeted at specific problems, but I do think we are being responsive to the concerns. I don't think we're ignoring them. But I think at the end of the day, the thing that's going to be most important to voters is going to be the state of the economy. And I do think that because of President Trump's leadership, you are seeing an investment boom. I mean, again, like I mentioned $650 billion of CAPEX in data centers just this year. That is just around the construction part of it; that doesn't include the economic activity that's being generated by the tokens inside the data center. And you're just starting to see that take off, like I mentioned with coding. Anthropic's revenue, which is all based on coding and enterprise, has scaled from something like 10 billion to 30 billion from January to March. It's really unprecedented. And with that, I mean, again, that's just their share of the economic activity. You have to think about well, who's buying those tokens? It's enterprises, it's small businesses, companies that are now building their own software and incorporating that into their business processes. So again, we're just at the outset of unleashing this business productivity explosion. And I understand the concerns about job loss and we're going to be paying attention to those things and really monitoring them, but again so far we do not see that in the data. I mean, you can say that it's going to happen in the future, but it has not happened yet. Yeah, the other really interesting thing that's happening in the political space with AI is there's the rise of these pro-AI Super PACs that have become like quite influential. I mean, how big is this? Do you think AI is sort of the next major power player in politics right now? Well, you're seeing PACs on both sides of the issue. So I'm seeing some very well-funded PACs on, you know, I call it kind of the doomer side of the issue. There's a spectrum, right? The doomer-boomer spectrum, yeah. Yeah, oh I don't know, I'm definitely not a boomer so I don't know is there like a Gen X version? But in any event, I think some of these doomer groups just want the progress to stop entirely. A lot of them have astroturfed some of the NIMBY backlash where, again, they don't want us building any new data centers, period. I think that would be excessive. There are these groups that are very well-funded that really do think that, you know, we're headed for the Terminator and they just want the progress to stop. And I do think that has affected the public discourse on this. Some of those groups that, I mean, some of them have billions of dollars in funding, they've funded journalism scholarships; like there's one called Tarbell where you have this like doomer think tank that funds journalists. It's kind of insane. But I do think it has a huge impact on the public discourse. Well the pro-AI Super PACs are getting into the game too though. Sure, I mean both sides are getting into the game. I mean look, anytime you've got a technology that is so transformative and profound, obviously you're going to get, you know, political activity around it. All right, David, thank you so so much for joining the conversation. This was fascinating, hope to do it again. All right, thanks Dasha. This has been The Conversation with Dasha Burns. We'll be back next week. If you want to catch future episodes of The Conversation, be sure to click that subscribe button below. Thanks for watching.

Trump on dissatisfaction with Iran, potential Spirit bailout, King Charles: Full press conference

USA TODAY(7.5M)·22:37·293 views·2026-05-01
Donald TrumpPresident
TL;DRPresident Trump expressed dissatisfaction with current negotiation terms with a "disjointed" Iranian leadership, while emphasizing a "USA First" economic agenda characterized by aggressive EU auto tariffs, record domestic job growth, and potential federal intervention to save 14,000 jobs at Spirit.

TL;DR

President Trump expressed dissatisfaction with current negotiation terms with a "disjointed" Iranian leadership, while emphasizing a "USA First" economic agenda characterized by aggressive EU auto tariffs, record domestic job growth, and potential federal intervention to save 14,000 jobs at Spirit.

Iran and Middle East Security

  • Negotiation Status: Iran reportedly seeks a deal due to a decimated military, but the President remains "not satisfied" with current terms.
      • Disjointed Leadership: The President characterized Iranian leadership as divided into three or four argumentative groups, making cohesive negotiation difficult.
      • Military Capability: Trump asserted that Iran’s navy, air force, and radar capabilities have been "wiped out" and its military "defeated."
  • Nuclear Non-Proliferation: A primary red line remains the prevention of Iran acquiring a nuclear weapon, which the President stated would cause "problems the likes of which the world has never seen."
  • Strategic Blockade: The Strait is described as "100 percent shut down" via a "flawless" blockade, which has trapped record amounts of oil in tankers.
  • Regional Support: Trump expressed strong support for the new head of Iraq and maintained "tremendous respect" for the leadership in Pakistan.

Trade and Domestic Manufacturing

  • EU Auto Tariffs: Tariffs on cars and trucks from the European Union were raised to 25% because the EU was "not adhering" to existing trade agreements.
      • Impact: The administration claims these tariffs force foreign manufacturers to move production to the U.S. more quickly.
  • Manufacturing Boom: Over $100 billion in new car plants are currently under construction in the U.S. by companies from Japan, South Korea, Canada, and Mexico.
  • China Relations: The President previewed an upcoming "amazing" trip to China to meet with Chairman Xi, noting that negotiations are ongoing.

Economic Policy and Corporate Assistance

  • Corporate Bailouts: The administration is evaluating a deal to help Spirit (Spirit AeroSystems) to save 14,000 jobs, though Trump emphasized that any deal must be "America First" and provide a return to the U.S.
      • Intel Precedent: Trump cited a previous deal with Intel that allegedly resulted in a $40 billion profit for the United States.
  • Tax Proposals: The President highlighted several key tax initiatives intended to stimulate the economy:
      • No tax on tips or overtime pay.
      • No tax on Social Security for seniors.
      • Tax deductions for interest on loans for American-made cars.
  • Energy Prices: While acknowledging high gasoline prices, the President predicted they would "tumble" significantly once current conflicts end and the oil currently trapped in tankers is released.

Military Authority and Defense

  • War Powers Act: The President dismissed the War Powers Act as "unconstitutional" and "never adhered to" by previous presidents, arguing against its use to restrict his authority.
  • Military Inventory: Trump criticized the Biden administration for giving away military inventory to Ukraine without compensation. He claimed the U.S. military is currently "locked and loaded" with double the inventory held at the start of the current period.
  • UK Relations: Trump spoke warmly of a "high-quality" meeting with King Charles and the Queen, praising the King’s previous work and their current relationship.
"Iran wants to make a deal because they have no military left, essentially... but at this moment, I'm not satisfied with what they're saying." — President Donald Trump
"We have a trade deal with the European Union. They were not adhering to it, so I raised the tariffs on cars and trucks to 25 percent. That's billions of dollars coming into the United States." — President Donald Trump
"USA first, America first. That’s my point, including with airlines, including with Intel... we're looking at Spirit, and if we can help them we will, but we have to come first." — President Donald Trump
"As soon as the war ends, gasoline prices are going to tumble because there is so much right now on the scene already loaded into tankers that can't escape the strait." — President Donald Trump
So they want to make a deal, but I don't—I'm not satisfied with it, so we'll see what happens. Iran wants to make a deal because they have no military left, essentially, and they want to make a deal, but I'm not satisfied with it. Because it's never been sought before. There's been numerous, many, many times, and nobody's ever gotten it before. They consider it totally unconstitutional. But we're always in touch with Congress. But nobody's ever sought it before, nobody's ever asked for it before. It's never been used before. Why should we be different? We have great respect for Pakistan and Islamabad, and tremendous respect for the Prime Minister and the Field Marshal, and they're working with us. They continue to work with us, but the trip is a very long one, and we're doing everything in terms of the negotiation right now telephonically. They've made strides, but I'm not sure if they ever get there. There's tremendous discord, there's tremendous—they're having a tremendous problem getting along with each other in Iran. The leadership is very disjointed. It's got two to three groups, maybe four, and it's a very disjointed leadership. And with that being said, they all want to make a deal, but they're all messed up. I spoke with your winner, a very good man. As you know, I was very strongly in favor of him in Iraq. And we had a long conversation yesterday. I congratulated him. He won, and with our help, he won, and we want him to do very well. I told him that the United States is with him all the way. It was a great victory. The new head of Iraq is somebody that we support very strongly. I know everything about it, yeah. I have great—I have great respect for Pakistan and for the Field Marshal and for the Prime Minister. Yeah, I know who they are. Well, I'm not happy with Italy and I'm not happy with Spain. They feel it's okay for Iran to have a nuclear weapon. Anybody that feels it's okay for Iran to have a nuclear weapon is not very smart, and it'll be a terrible thing in the future if they were ever allowed to have a nuclear weapon. You would have problems the likes of which the world has never seen before, and it's not going to happen. And aside from that, right now they have no navy—it's been wiped out. No air force—it's been wiped out. They have no anything. They have no anti-aircraft, they have no radar. They have no leaders, because frankly, their leaders are very disjointed. They have a lot of problems right now. They're not getting along with each other. We raised the tariffs on cars coming in from the European Union because the European Union was not adhering to the trade deal we have. So based on that now, when they build their plants, of which they're spending over a hundred billion dollars for countries, not just the Union—when those plants open, there won't be any tariffs. But we raised the tariffs because they were not—as usual, they were not adhering to the agreement that we have. We have a trade deal with the European Union. They were not adhering to it, so I raised the tariffs on cars and trucks to 25 percent. That's billions of dollars coming into the United States, and it forces them to move their factory production much faster. We have right now in the United States over a hundred billion dollars of car plants being built. It's a record. We've never had anything like it from all countries: Japan, South Korea, every—by the way, Canada, Mexico. They're all building plants in the United States. But the European Union was not adhering to the deal that we made. As you know, it's a very disjointed, unfriendly leadership—unfriendly to each other. They're not getting along. They're very, very confused by what's happened because essentially most of their military's been wiped out. But we just had a conversation with Iran. Let's see what happens. But I would say that I am not happy. The leadership is disjointed, very argumentative with each other. They come back; one says one thing, one says another. They're very confused. Obviously, their country has been frankly decimated. Their navy is gone, their air force is gone. Many of their soldiers, unfortunately, are gone. They've got to come up with the right deal. At this moment, I'm not satisfied with what they're saying. Well, I guess we're looking at it. If we could do it, we'd do it, but only if it's a good deal. We're looking at it, but if we can't make a good deal, no institution's been able to do it. I said I'd like to save the jobs, but we'll have an announcement sometime today. We gave them a final proposal. No other country has ever done it. It's never been, as you know—most people consider it totally unconstitutional. Also, we had a ceasefire, so that gives you additional time. But no other country's done it. We're in the midst of a big victory. This is a victory like we haven't had since Venezuela, okay, to be honest with you. We haven't had—but you know, we have a country that lost its way. It really lost its way. That's our country, in terms of the military. Now we have a great military. We're on our way to another victory, a big victory. And I don't think that it's constitutional what they're asking for. These are not patriotic people that are asking. You know, when they say—even the losers, even the ones that say all the wrong things admit that it's been amazing what we've done. The strait is totally shut down. It's flawless. It's totally 100 percent shut down. Now, we've been dealing with Iran. They want to make a deal. They're not there. They're not with the—they're sort of—they get close and then a new group of people come in. They don't know who their leaders are. They have no idea who their leaders are. But they're very confused. And that's because of the success we've had militarily. They essentially have no military. Let me just tell you the War Powers... So many presidents, as you know, have gone and exceeded it. It's never been used. It's never been adhered to. And every other president considered it totally unconstitutional, and we agree with that. No, the China—it's going to be amazing. The visit with China and Chairman Xi, that's going to be great. We have the trip with China coming up, and it'll be—I think it'll be an amazing event. Well, right now we have negotiations going on. They're not getting there. They are a very disjointed—they're extremely disjointed. They're not able to get along with each other as leaders. They don't know who the leader is. Their military's been defeated. We've had a—if we left right now, it would take them 20 years to build back that country. But we're not satisfied at this moment. Why would I tell you that? We're looking at trying to help them—something we're not looking to get involved with, but if we can, it's 14,000 jobs. We are—I would say we're driving a tough deal, but it's one of those things. We'll do it or we won't. We'll have something for you today or tomorrow. Well, they will, you know, but we come first. Hey, you know what it is? USA first, America first. That's my point, including with airlines, including with Intel. You know, we made an Intel deal. You know that, right? We made about 40 billion dollars on that, and someday Fox and Peter's going to talk about it. But we helped out Intel, and we ended up making 40 billion dollars. So we're looking at Spirit—different, a different kind of a thing. But we're looking at Spirit and if we can help them we will, but we have to come first. We're first. It's America first. Well, only that they had a very good meeting. Well, there are options. I mean, do we want to go and just blast the hell out of them and finish them forever, or do we want to try and make a deal? I mean, those are the options. I'd prefer not. On a human basis, I'd prefer not. But that's the options: do we want to go in there heavy and just blast them away, or do we want to do something? They are a very disjointed leadership, as you can understand, Peter, very disjointed. I mean, they're not getting along with each other. And it puts us in a bad position. One group wants to make a certain deal, the other group wants to make a certain deal, including the hardliners. The hardliners want to make a deal too. Why wouldn't they? They have no navy, they have no air force, they have no anti-aircraft—they have no nothing. No, no. We have more than we've ever had, actually. Because all over the world we have inventory and we can take that if we need it. But all over the world we have tremendous amounts of inventory, the vast... For instance, we're stocked and locked and loaded. Right now, we have more than double what we had when this started. But you know, we're all over the world. We're in many, many different countries, many different places, all packed up. Now what happened is Biden came in like a stupid fool and he did—he gave a lot of what we had to Ukraine. Right. And the 350 billion dollars they spent. We don't do that. Everybody has to pay. The European Union pays us. But Biden didn't get money. He got nothing. He got nothing. But he gave a lot of the inventory away. Now, relatively speaking, it's not that much, but it was a lot of inventory. He gave it to Ukraine. We've built it up. But we have inventory from all over the world. Probably. No, no. The King and I had a great—he's just a great person. He's a high-quality person. I love the job he did in Congress. He actually got the Democrats to stand. He's a—he's a wonderful person. She's a wonderful lady, a wonderful Queen, and we had a good time. Yeah, they're going to be coming. I don't even think about it, Peter. Oh, I think Pete Rose is great. I think Pete Rose should have been in the Baseball Hall of Fame a long time ago. A long time ago before his death. I think baseball did a great disservice by not respecting his achievements on the field. He was a great player. I knew him well. He was a great player. The blockade has been unbelievable. Powerful, 100 percent. It's been actually unbelievable. If we left right now, we had a great victory, but we're not doing that. We're negotiating with them. Their teams are unbelievably disjointed. But we are negotiating. The stock market is hitting record numbers. We have more people working in the USA today than we've ever had working, as you know. It's—it's in many ways... Now, when this war ends, gasoline and oil and everything is going to come tumbling down. But right now, as of this moment, the stock market has hit record highs. We have more people, again, working in the United States today than at any time in the history of our country. We are really doing well. Now, gasoline's high, other prices are way down, but gasoline's high. But when this is over, you'll have a world without a nuclear weapon with Iran. If you allow Iran to have a nuclear weapon, the world would be in great jeopardy, so it's not going to happen. As soon as the—as soon as the war ends, the gasoline prices will tumble. As soon as the war ends, gasoline prices are going to tumble because there is so much right now on the scene already loaded into tankers—tankers that can't escape the strait. There is so much, like record-setting. But when the war ends, gasoline prices will go down to below what they were. No tax on tips. We have—we have the largest tax cuts in the history of our country also, and regulation cuts, and it's one of the reasons we're doing so well. So we have no tax on tips, no tax on social security for our seniors. We have no tax on overtime. And you know, we... something that nobody talks about. If you buy a car, if it's American-made, you get a deduction on interest for income tax purposes. Right over there, the greatest—you're going to have the greatest ballroom ever built. It's on time, on budget, and it's doing great. Well, they have some of the greatest champions in the world. The arena, they're going to start building it over the next week. It's right here; it'll be about 5,000 seats. They'll have 100,000 people down by the Ellipse with screens and it's all free. It's going to be great. I got it. Again, we have more people working in the United States today than ever before in history. Outside of the gasoline, prices are way down—prices have just come down, mostly because I inherited that. We inherited the highest inflation in the history of our country, 48 years. In the history of our country. And as—prices are down, when the war ends, and it shouldn't be too long, they want to make a deal so badly, but they're not there yet; they're not there in my opinion. When the war ends, gasoline prices are going to tumble because there is so much right now on the scene already loaded into tankers—tankers that can't escape the strait. There is so much, like record-setting. But when the war ends, gasoline prices will go down to below what they were. The stock market just hit an all-time high, and again, more people working today than at any time ever. And we just got the largest tax cut in history with the no tax... No, I liked him a lot, so he's—nobody's yet but I liked him a lot. You like it? That's good. I love you, Mr. President. Thank you, thank you, Mr. President. I'm not happy with the delivery of the weapons. I'm not thrilled with it. But a small amount of weapons were sent, and we'll see who has them. But I'm not happy with what happened with the Kurds. The Kurds did not deliver the weapons.

OpenAI Misses Targets, Codex vs Claude, Elon vs Sam Trial, Big Hyperscaler Beats, Peptide Craze

All-In Podcast(1.1M)·80:57·230.2K views·2026-05-01
Chamath PalihapitiyaCo-host, All-In Podcast Jason CalacanisCo-host, All-In Podcast David SacksCo-host, All-In Podcast
TL;DR"Everything in this market is power-constrained.
Big Tech is undergoing a massive structural shift toward an asset-heavy "industrial" model, with CAPEX projections hitting $1 trillion as companies scramble for power and compute, while OpenAI faces internal friction over missed growth targets despite maintaining a...

TL;DR: Big Tech is undergoing a massive structural shift toward an asset-heavy "industrial" model, with CAPEX projections hitting $1 trillion as companies scramble for power and compute, while OpenAI faces internal friction over missed growth targets despite maintaining a product edge over a compute-constrained Anthropic.
