How are these people able to make a fulsome argument in like, one side gets thirty minutes, the other side gets thirty minutes, there's a little Q&A and then you're done in an hour? There's this whole art and science, and Sacks you're probably familiar with this, on how do you distill down a Supreme Court case in the briefing doc. Like what is it you're petitioning around the court and you try and distill it down to the exact legal interpretation you want the judges to rule on. Not all the other bullshit. And this is oral arguments, yes. Oral arguments, just a discussion and then the judges jump in and all they're doing is asking the lawyer questions. One lawyer at a time. The one side and then the other side. And by the way the Solicitor General came up in the middle and kind of made a few comments and they asked her some questions from the White House and she sat down. And then the two sides kind of went back and forth and they just did thirty minutes Q&A each on that one specific legal question. And Ketanji Brown Jackson said, "But what if after the EPA issued the label they found out information that it does cause cancer? Shouldn't they update the label?" And he's saying, "Well no they're not allowed to, they can only issue the label the EPA says." And he says also, "And it's a criminal case if they find out that it does cause cancer and they don't report it to the EPA." And then she's saying, "Well what if the EPA doesn't act and shouldn't the states have a right to protect their people?" So those are the legal arguments, the discussions that are going on in all of this. And there's interesting implications which is fundamentally if the states get to interpret federal law and ignore federal regulatory bodies like the EPA, it opens up a whole new can of worms in terms of like all the states can start to ignore federal regulatory bodies. Like the EPA or the FDA or the USDA and on and on and on. So the whole case has a whole bunch of really interesting implications wound up in it. When you hear these guys and they're just talking, Chamath, about that exact interpretation of the law and that's what this comes down to, it's not the actual case that matters. And after this, Sacks, they go from oral arguments and then they have like a private conference where they'll write their papers and give their final judgment, yeah? Sacks? Yeah, I think what happens is that some, I guess, discussion happens behind closed doors and they figure out where the majority is. And then the Chief gets to assign who writes the opinion for the majority. In that meeting nobody is allowed in. And in fact you have a double door system where like if anything needs to come in and out you have to like kind of like knock on the door, you're let into this antechamber then... Oh is it an airlock? It's effectively... we, I had, I don't know if you were there Jason, but we had Ted Cruz come to play in the poker game. I wasn't back then. And Ted Cruz clerked for William Rehnquist. And if you want to have an incredible dinner ask him about the Supreme Court and Bill Rehnquist. He's a real student of the Supreme Court and it just makes the Supreme Court, Friedberg, sound like the most incredible body that's ever been created anywhere. By the way, more than the White House, more than the Capitol building, more than any of these other big agencies, this place has... it's almost like being in England. It has these kind of ways that people operate. The security is so different. They kind of stand there in the court and they all exchange places every twenty minutes. It's very coordinated; they're dressed very differently than any other... How many people are in the courtroom listening? Maybe like 150 I would say. How do you get tickets? Are they on StubHub? So I think... I actually think everyone is a guest of a clerk or someone that works at the court. I don't think that it's like very publicly available to get in there. You can't line up? There's no line-up? There's, I don't know if there's a line-up. This was a connection through the... We got in through the Chief Justice. He gave us the pass. But I think it was like very... I think at the Elon versus OpenAI case there's... you can line up and then the judge gave like 30 tickets to the press and selected them. But that's not Supreme Court. That's a different court. No, no, yeah, but I think there's a line-up for the Supreme Court as well. There's some public access that they're entitled to. It did not look like anyone from the public was in this court. Everyone is dressed... maybe it's a lottery. Everyone is dressed respectfully. I mean, this court has an incredible amount of like kind of behavior... That's a super cool experience. That's really awesome. Enjoy it while you can. I mean I think the Supreme Court is one of the last highly functional institutions in the United States and... 100 percent. ...you know, at some point we're going to have like 13 or 21 or some crazy number of justices up there. They should put in an arena, Sacks, and get jerseys and everybody show up with their jerseys. Yeah the court's going to get packed Sacks and it's going to be really a rough-and-tumble of justices there and so enjoy it while it's still in the current form it's in. Can you imagine? Sacks I hope you're wrong. Can you imagine showing up with jerseys with the justices' names on them and like having sections and somebody selling Cracker Jacks? It would be the idiocracy version of the Supreme Court. Exactly. The popularity of the court really depends on whether it's issuing decisions that people agree with. That's what it comes down to. If like if you ask people whether they like the Supreme Court or not, it really just depends on whether they agree with the decisions they're issuing. Recency. As opposed to the process of the decisions and how well-argued it is and all these things that you're pointing to. And actually the court... I mean I just checked the numbers. The court is relatively popular right now. I think that it got as low as 35 percent in the 2024 Gallup survey, but I think it's back up to, you know, like 44 to 50 percent favorability. Which for something that's involved in politics is relatively high, right? Like you look at Congress or any particular politician, they're going to be lower than that typically. I just felt so assured of like the institution when I visited and saw these guys interact and behave and how they behave the process. It was like man this is what... what an amazing country. Yeah. Well anyway... Well the reason I say what I say is there was an interview with James Carville recently. Did you guys see this? Where he said... I saw that. I saw that. ...he said "Look, when we get power, we're packing the court." He said "We're not even going to worry about it, and we're going to make it 13." Yeah, he said get it to 13. Yeah I think yeah they're going to go from 9 to 13 and then they're going to create some new states and all the rest of it. So that will be that. Enjoy it while it lasts. Enjoy it while it lasts. Way to end on a high note. Way to yeah see the end of the empire. That'll be that. By the way, there is a Supreme Court... I was correct, there is an online ticketing lottery, so we can all sign up and you can get a four-pack of tickets. I think they should make this... I think we should talk to Howard Lutnick, maybe he can make this an auction we get a revenue stream for the US. We can sell like 10 of the tickets as courtside seats for 20 grand. Jason you're exactly what they're trying to protect against happening in that courtroom. Exactly. How can we, how can we monetize the Supreme Court? All right, everybody that's it. That's the world's greatest podcast for you. For Chamath Palihapitiya, David Friedberg, and David Sacks, I am the world's greatest moderator. We'll see you next time. You feel like the Chief Justice. I'm like the Chief Justice of the All-In Podcast.

Jensen Huang & Anirudh Devgan on Design for AI and AI for Design at CadenceLIVE 2026 – Fireside Chat

Cadence Design Systems(41.6K)·34:22·74 views·2026-05-01
Jensen HuangFounder and CEO, NVIDIA Anirudh DevganPresident and CEO, Cadence
TL;DRThe industry is shifting from generative AI that "knows" to agentic AI that "does," effectively expanding the chip design workforce by 1,000x as virtual agents use specialized EDA tools to solve complex optimization and physical design problems.
The "Knowing" vs. "Doing"...

TL;DR

The industry is shifting from generative AI that "knows" to agentic AI that "does," effectively expanding the chip design workforce by 1,000x as virtual agents use specialized EDA tools to solve complex optimization and physical design problems.

The Shift from Generative to Agentic AI

  • The "Knowing" vs. "Doing" Inflection Point: Jensen Huang emphasized that while generative AI (perception and knowledge) was the first step, the current "agentic" revolution allows AI to reason, execute plans, and use external tools.
  • The "Microwave" Analogy for Tool Use: Huang argued that agents will not reinvent fundamental engineering algorithms but will instead use them as tools.
      • Just as a household robot would use a microwave rather than emitting radiation from its fingers, AI agents will use Cadence’s established EDA tools to verify and refine designs.
  • Scaling Beyond Human Constraints: Historically, the semiconductor industry has been limited by the number of human designers ("butts in seats"). In the agentic era, growth is limited only by compute and token budgets.

AI-Driven Specialized Chip Design Agents

  • Domain-Specific Agents: NVIDIA and Cadence are collaborating on several specialized agents to handle the most labor-intensive parts of chip design:
      • ChipStack: Focuses on RTL (Register Transfer Level) and verification, specializing in complex protocols like PCIe Express and CXL.
      • VeraStack: An agent for analog design, which has traditionally been difficult to automate due to the extreme limits of physics and signal integrity.
  • Physical Design Optimization: Physical design is described as a "many-variable, impossible-to-find-a-perfect-answer" search algorithm.
      • Agents allow for massive parallelization, running thousands of permutations 24/7 to find the best PPA (Power, Performance, Area) solutions.
  • Virtual Engineers: Anirudh Devgan noted that virtual agents are already integrated into NVIDIA’s workflows, participating in Slack channels alongside human engineers to solve specific design bugs.

The Rise of Physical AI and Robotics

  • The "ChatGPT Moment" for Robotics: The industry has arrived at the Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model era. This allows for "pixels-in, action-out" capabilities, enabling robots to understand physicality and reason through complex scenarios.
  • Synthetic Data and World Models: Because physical data is harder to gather than text, NVIDIA and Cadence are using "AI Physics" to generate synthetic data.
      • High-Fidelity Simulation: Cadence’s acquisition of MSC (Adams) provides the high-fidelity physics solvers needed to train NVIDIA’s Cosmos and Isaac platforms.
      • Reasoning in Motion: Huang highlighted "Alpha Mo," a reasoning car that can "think out loud" to navigate complex environments (e.g., deciding to move around a truck parked in its lane).

Post-Agentic Leadership and Business Strategy

  • The Token Budget Paradigm: Huang predicts that future engineering productivity will be measured by token usage.
      • If a team isn't spending hundreds of millions—or even billions—of tokens on agents, they are likely failing to maximize their scope of responsibility.
  • The "Full-Stack" Company: Devgan and Huang agreed that engineering is no longer siloed. Designing a modern chip requires a full-stack understanding of the software, the systems, and even the "AI factories" (data centers) where they reside.
  • Expanded TAM for EDA: By moving from chips into physical systems (robotics, automotive, CFD), Cadence is expanding its Total Addressable Market (TAM) to include every industry that designs physical products.
"People who are 'know-it-alls' are interesting but not valuable. People who can do things are valuable. AI went from knowing everything... to now being able to use tools." — Jensen Huang
"The number of ASIC designers is likely to increase by a thousand times in the near future... In the future, we’re only limited by compute." — Jensen Huang
"One thing remarkable about chip design is how much effort we put in verification... it takes one person to design it, and 100 engineers to fix it. [With agents], my Slack channel will be 99% Cadence." — Anirudh Devgan
"If a company isn't spending billions of dollars of tokens a year, obviously we’re not going to be producing the things that we’re doing. These are new sensibilities that are coming in." — Jensen Huang
Please welcome Cadence President and CEO, Dr. Anirudh Devgan. Hello everyone. Morning. Welcome to CadenceLIVE. We have a super exciting program this year. This year, actually, we went to two days given all the interest. But to kick it off, we have a phenomenal keynote session. Again, we are kicking off with my good friend, Jensen, who's here—a person who needs no introduction. You know, he is the Godfather of AI himself. Please welcome Jensen to the stage. Anirudh! Ladies and gentlemen, Anirudh. Jensen, you want to start... Hey, this is the M2000. Oh, is that right? Oh, we built this computer together. Yes, yes. Three tons! Alright, am I signing here? Yeah, yeah. That computer is going to be worth—that's going to be worth a hundred times more in a few years. Okay, Jensen. Alright, great to see you guys. Which side am I? This side. This side. Yeah. Also, the other super exciting thing in '24 is now we have a lot more of our products running on the Millennium 2000 with NVIDIA. Well, we're going to have everything running. Yeah, we're going to accelerate everything. I mean, this is such an extraordinary time. You guys know that Anirudh is the only CEO in the world that grew up in high-performance computing, who grew up in algorithms, and who knew about the capabilities of CUDA long before. Yeah, it's really incredible. It's a great partnership, Jensen. Great partnership. Right. And it's really remarkable to see what NVIDIA has become—the most valuable company in the world. Of course, you're transforming multiple industries. So last year, we talked about how generative AI was just coming on, right? And of course, you had a fabulous keynote last month at GTC. But as you look back at the evolution of AI over the last 6 to 12 months, what do you see in the next 6 to 12 months? Yeah, in fact, if you just go back, it literally was only two years. And in two years' time, we went from generative AI... this whole thing started kicking off with generative AI and ChatGPT. Generative AI was such a big deal because it was able to—of course, we solved perception long before that—but generative AI was a big deal because it was able to generate tokens. This is foundational to two things. Number one is the ability to think, right? You can't think if you can't create words or generate words in your mind. And then number two, it is the ability to interact with other tools, external tools. And so it set the foundation for reasoning, which was next, and then agentic AI. Generative AI and ChatGPT really created the foundation of that. We're now at a point where agents are able to perceive, reason, and execute plans. The reason why this is such a big deal is because AI and intelligence is interesting. People who are "know-it-alls," who understand it all—know-it-alls are interesting but not valuable. People who can do things are valuable. And so AI went from knowing everything, being able to spew out all kinds of knowledge and information, to now being able to use tools. Which is why it's such an incredible time for Cadence and such an incredible time for the industry, because we can now have AI that use tools. And if you ever profile it, we use agents all over our company. Our token use is going exponential. And if you profile what the agents are doing, the moment they reason about what they have to do, they spin off tools, tools, tools, tools, tools. And we've got all kinds of agents. We're working with you on chip design agents. And we're going to have all kinds of ASIC designer agents, whether it's verification, which is the first one, ChipStack, or analog chip designers and back-end chip designers. We're going to have all kinds of agents. And the thing that's going to be incredible is that the tool use of the EDA industry—the tool use of Cadence—is just going to skyrocket. Yeah, that's actually a very good point. Because you know, there's this worry the last few months in the market that, "Oh, AI is going to replace tool use," right? So this is... Yeah, that's right. And there are two... you know, of course, software companies are now categorized. You need to categorize software companies a little bit better. There are some software companies that are focused on workflow automation. Workflow automation, of course, you could argue that a lot of that is going to be augmented by AI. But Cadence, and many software companies—particularly engineering and EDA—the software is not workflow automation; it's fundamental tools. These algorithms are not going to be... you're not going to rediscover a better one. These are excellent algorithms that have been refined over time and they're fundamentally grounded. It's integrated into all of our companies and we can't have agents coming up with random other ones. We want them to use the tools that we have. And so the tool use, in this case, agentic systems—this agent revolution and this inflection point of inference—is going to drive adoption and drive consumption of tools. Your industry and my industry is limited by one thing, which is the number of ASIC designers we have. In my case, the number of ASIC designers we have limits how many products we could create. In your case, the number of ASIC designers I have limits how many tools, tool use, and tool licenses, if you will. Well, the number of ASIC designers is likely to increase by a thousand times in the near future. And so this is going to be an extraordinary time for tool industries like yours. By the way, you had a great analogy. Remember we were talking a few weeks ago and you had this robot analogy coming to your house? I love that analogy—that you know, if you have a robot come to your house and it has to warm up some food, do you do it with your hands or do you use a microwave that's already there? Yeah, tool use. Yeah, the ultimate expression of agentic systems. We have agents which are digital robots, and we're going to have physical robots that we're working on. Suppose we just created the world's most amazing physical robot and they come to our house. Are we going to create a physical robot that has microwaves beaming out of its fingers? You know, or its hands turn into a hundred-pound hammer? Or are they just going to pick up a hammer? You know, use the microwave, use the dishwasher? They're going to use tools. And so tool use in agentic systems is defined by the words "tool use." They're going to use existing tools. And we need to be grounded on those tools because in the end I've got to know whether the answer's right. And I know how to use Cadence. And so if it uses Cadence and I use Cadence and everything is put back into Cadence, then I can come back and I can verify it, I could collaborate with it, and I could fine-tune it, you know, so on and so forth. And Jensen, it's great to work... of course, you are always ahead of... you know, the one thing remarkable about you is not just the AI outside, but you're driving the industry. Also, look at how much you apply inside to improve your own work. And of course, we have worked with NVIDIA for a long time to always try to improve the design process. And so, like, we announced ChipStack, as you may know, a couple of months ago with NVIDIA. And like Jensen, you're saying, the first use case was RTL and verification, because that's kind of a natural thing. And this agent—this agent knows PCIe Express protocol, which is extremely complex, by the way—and memory interface protocols and CXL protocols, and basically the APIs of chips. This agent specializes in the language of chips and understands all the complicated protocols. It's built on top of that understanding so that it could do verification with us. Yeah, it's really incredible. Nothing is more frustrating than a PCIe Express bug. You know, you chase that one forever. Do you guys agree? You know, one thing remarkable about chip design is how much effort we put in verification because, of course, in the software industry, it is 100 to 1. Yeah, it takes one person to design it and 100 engineers to fix it. And that's something that the software industry doesn't do as much, right? Like pure software. But in chip design, with RTL design, the verification part is super critical. Yeah, so of course we are very glad to work with you and your team on ChipStack. But now in the last few months... We have a bunch of... we have a bunch of engineers, they have all these different names, and then we have a bunch of engineers named "Cadence." Yeah, exactly. And they're on Slack channels with us. Slack channels with us. Virtual engineers. Virtual engineers. And I love all the email addresses that start with Cadence. You know, they do exactly what I want. Yeah, exactly. But Jensen, even the real engineers from Cadence... I work with them 24/7. In the future, my Slack channel will be 99% Cadence. Yeah, thank you. Yeah. I got Cadence agents talking to other Cadence agents. By the way, even the real Cadence employees do what you want, not just the agents. I got you. Thank you. Thank you. Now on the... recently, more recently, you know, we have two new super agents that we're working on with NVIDIA. Of course, the back-end—because what I keep hearing from customers, especially NVIDIA—is that you have this amazing Cadence now every year and the chips get bigger, so the amount of PD work goes up in the back-end. And then the other big thing is analog. You know, analog design has always been very difficult—very difficult to automate. So we have this new Virtuoso agent, VeraStack, that we work on with your team. So I'm super excited about what happened in these two new areas apart from ChipStack. Yeah, those are really great choices. The challenge with physical design... you know, there's an impression for people that are outside the industry that we design the chip and then at some point you have a netlist and you take that netlist and you do place and route. Well, these chips are so large. You know, if you think about chips the way you think about cities, each one of our chips is like all the cities in the world combined. And you're basically doing city planning while the buildings are being created, while everything's being designed. And so we plan physical design from practically the beginning of the design process because that's how long it takes. You're in the physical design process for two years. And the reason for that is because of course the amount of technology that... the amount of components that fit into the physical design that you have to place is large. But you have a fundamental bin-packing problem and you're trying to optimize for size, you're optimizing for manufacturability, and you're optimizing for timing. Meanwhile, everything's being designed at the same time. It's an incredibly challenging process. And so now we have agentic systems that help us basically work 24/7. You parallelize the work across thousands of agents. And I'm hoping—and today we're scaling it up to tens and hundreds—but we'd like to scale it up to thousands and hundreds of thousands of agents. They'll all be working around the clock trying all kinds of different permutations so that you could solve this many-variable, impossible-to-find-a-perfect-answer optimization problem. And so this is an ideal place for agents. Analog design is very similar to that. The design space is extremely large and it's hard to know when you're pushing the limits of analog design. We're basically at the limits of physics. And there's a lot of different permutations, a lot of different ways you could design the analog circuitry. Having agents explore the space for us, simultaneously designing for signal integrity, for power, for size, and for manufacturability, exploring all of that simultaneously across all the agents. These are all exploration problems. And so I think the... there's a misunderstanding about fundamental design that you have a problem statement, you come up with a solution like you're solving a puzzle, and that's the answer. Well, it turns out you know there's a thousand—exactly—plausible answers, a million plausible answers. The question is which one are you going to go and optimize towards? Well, having more agents helping you explore the design space, the better. And that has been the history of EDA and chip design. Because like you've said before, there is no one right answer. There are thousands of right answers. You have to find the one with the best PPA. So anything that helps find the best right answer is invaluable in the design process. That's right. It's a search algorithm, and one of the most complex search algorithms in the world. We're going to need a lot of agents to help us do that. Yeah, super exciting. So Jensen, also, you talked about at GTC... by the way, that was a great keynote. There's a warning to all the companies: your tool use is going to skyrocket; you better secure your licenses soon. Call your Cadence salesperson right away. Yeah, like tool... which I think we all know. The number of computers is going to skyrocket, the number of licenses of software tools are going to skyrocket. Agents... we've historically been limited, both of us have been limited by butts in seats. In the future, we're only limited by compute. And so more virtual teams. That's right. And one thing you mentioned in your keynote, which was very interesting, of course—the explosion of inference that is caused by agents. But also you said, "Oh, in the future, every company or every employee, you would give them a token budget or like an automation budget," because they will be assisted by agents and tools. So that's a very different thinking. You know, you want to make sure the employees we have become super employees with the help of agents and more. So how do you put that into practice? I think it'll be good for other companies to know too. Well, you know, right now across the company we're encouraging everyone to use Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor. Those are the three most important things to us. And in the case of... and we're getting extraordinary success with Codex. Codex 2 is coming and so I'm super excited about that. We have early access to that, and that's going to work incredibly well. Claude Code, as we know, is completely revolutionary. And so every single engineer, if you're not using one of those three tools, we got to ask you... I got to ask you why you're not using one of those three tools. And these agents... and we're seeing great success all over the place. Zero-touch automation across so many different domains. But the one thing that's happening, of course, is that every engineer is orchestrating a whole bunch of agents which are using a whole bunch of sub-agents. And so for example, ChipStack would be a sub-agent that we're calling on to do verification. And Vera, and you know, right, all of those are going to be sub-agents that we're calling. And so the number of agents that we have solving problems, automating things... And yesterday I heard... you know, I won't describe too much what we're actually doing with it—converting from one codebase to another codebase, completely functional, zero touch. It would have taken 10 years, 10 engineers a year to do; it was literally done in five minutes... five hours... and completely zero touch. And we're seeing this across the company. Our token use of course is going skyrocketing, which is fantastic, because ultimately we wanted to hire more people anyways. And now we automate through agents, made every single engineer more productive. The net result of that is back in the old days you might ask your VLSI team, "Well, how many people do you have?" Not that more is better, but one is not enough. And so you know that. And so you kind of have a feeling for how large VLSI teams need to be, how large place and route teams need to be, how large your library team needs to be, and so on and so forth. And so you have a feeling for that based on the amount of work that you have to do. We have a complete reset now. Now, based on the people that we have, I want everybody to be maximizing the agents that they are orchestrating to automate their work. And if they have mastered the work of orchestrating agents, by definition, their token use is going to go up. And so at the end of the year, if somebody said, "I'm... my scope of responsibility is this, and my group used no tokens," that's no different than somebody coming up to me saying, "My VLSI team is going to build Feynman," and I say, "Oh yeah, how many VLSI engineers do we have?" "Eh, three." Right, I know that's obviously the wrong answer. And so you obviously either don't understand the problem or Feynman is not going to show up. And so I... we have the same... you need to have the same sensibility now about post-agentic leadership, post-agentic engineering, and post-agentic company building. If a company isn't spending hundred... in our case, if we're not spending... right now we're already spending hundreds and hundreds of millions of dollars of tokens a year. If we're not spending billions of dollars of tokens a year, obviously we're not going to be producing the things that we're doing. And so those are new sensibilities that are coming in. Right. And the other good thing with this agentic framework is of course we will have some specialized agents, like for RTL verification or analog, but NVIDIA can mix and match their agents with it, right? Because anything which is specific to NVIDIA or specific to Feynman... I'm super interested in artificial general intelligence; that's interesting. But I need specialized agents that are great at place and route, because that's my life. I need specialized agents that know how to design and verify; that's my life. And so in one case, I'm interested in it, I enjoy reading about it, I don't mind using it. On the other hand, our company has critical needs and those specialized agents have to be the world's best. And that... we're counting on you to build them. Exactly. No, we are ready, Jensen. So this will be the next great chapter in our collaboration is these new agentic flows. And also, you know, like we discussed a few months ago, we also recently acquired a physical AI simulation software from Hexagon. MSC. Yeah, MSC. Terrific. Good job. Thank you. It's one of the... Yeah, smart. It's one of the original solvers in the industry. That's right, multi-dynamic solver. Yeah. And then they have one of the great products for multi-body dynamics, you know, for robotics and cars and all that. And we worked with them before you bought them. Exactly, yeah, thank you. And then he buys them. I kind of figured it out. He's watching who I'm working with. Yeah, clever. Same thing, actually, you also... I'm watching who you're working with. Yeah. If you remember we also worked with this Stanford company, Cascade on CFD. Yeah, fantastic. We bought that one too. Yeah. Incredible, so smart. But you have talked a lot about physical AI. AI that understands physicality, understands the laws of dynamics, understands the laws of physics. And then the other one is AI physics, which is AI that's pretending to do physics, emulation of physics. One is physical AI, the other one is AI physics. And we started with agentic AI, just AI, now we're working on AI physics and physical AI. Exactly. Embedded agentic system. Incredible, physically embodied agents. And that's going to be huge. I know we talked about it for years. I think that could be huge. It could be as big or bigger than the first wave of all this physical AI. Well, the industry's larger. I mean, if you look at the world's hundred trillion dollar industry, the vast majority of it is physical AI. It has to understand physicality and where to do its work. And just as we had the ChatGPT moment—the generative AI moment for language—we've arrived at the generative AI moment for robotics. It's called VLA, Vision-Language-Action model, which basically is perception in, action out. Just as we had words in, words out, we now have words in, action out and pixels in, action out. So we now have the generative AI moment. And we include reasoning to it so that we embody this robot with human knowledge priors. So for example, it could do reasoning about things like, you know, if a ball's rolling across the street, don't go chase it. That reasoning system is embodied in our words somewhere. And so using chain of thought—the same reasoning systems that we created for language—you can now decompose a very complicated scenario into simple scenarios that we know how to perform. People who are good reasoners are able to encounter a brand-new circumstance that has never been experienced before, that you've never described or never learned from, but you can through reasoning. You can decompose it into simple things that you can address and you can solve. And so we've now embodied two things: generative capability into the robotic systems—VLA—and reasoning models into it so that it could be very functional even though trained on a relatively small corpus of previous knowledge, because a lot of that reasoning system is embodied in the language model. And so these two capabilities, now we combine that with advances in mechatronics—extremely good motors, and that's advancing incredibly fast—boom, robotic systems are here. And it's multi-embodiment. You can teach one AI model to deal with the whole body and it works with wheels, it works with two wheels, four wheels, one wheel... I mean it's incredible. Yeah, the AI just figures it out. Just like we learned how to ride a bicycle ourselves, we embody the bicycle. We learn how to drive a car, we embody the car. We can learn how to use a unicycle, we embody the unicycle. We've learned how to play tennis, we embody the tennis racket. We now have figured out how to do that generally. Artificial General Robotics, that modeling system we now understand. So yeah, physical AI has arrived. And you have a big effort on it, right? Huge effort. Yeah, yeah. And then I think one thing there, which of course... And so we're partnering on that. And so NVIDIA has a large team of engineers working with Cadence's large team of engineers and you're creating simulation systems and you're connecting it to your principal solvers which Cadence is world-class at. And so and of course the MSC team, we're working with you across the board on robotic systems. Exactly. Oh, that's super exciting. Because one thing like you know, the... and you know just for the audience, to train these VLA models, the data is not as readily available as it is to train LLM models. Because LLM models, the data is on the internet. But for physical data, either you have to capture it, which takes a lot of time, but the right way to generate is synthetic data—you know, like Cosmos and Omniverse and Isaac. But the synthetic data has to be more... the more accurate it is, the better the model will be. So what we're working together with NVIDIA, thanks for that, is to embed MSC Adams, which is a very accurate physics simulator, together with Cosmos. That's right. And so just imagine how you would teach someone how to play tennis or golf. The first thing, of course, they have to have general capability. They have general manipulation, general articulation capability. And so you train that, you develop that through one of two ways. One is through imitation, and that's where you use teleoperation or "watch me do something." And so that's imitation learning. The second part is creating synthetic data through principal solvers, which is the part that we're working on. And the principal solvers simulate essentially articulation, manipulation, so on and so forth. And it's all physically based because it obeys the laws of physics. So inertia is obeyed, friction is obeyed, contact is obeyed, and so on and so forth. And the second part is simulation. And the third part, we use these two capabilities and we train a world foundation model. And that world foundation model, trained well enough, at some point could start generating synthetic data. And this is no different than as I'm explaining this, you imagine how you learn how to do things. You learn through imitation. You learn through people explaining the first-principle logic and mechanics to you, and then at some point you might even just dream about it. You're simulating in your mind, "If I did this, what would happen? If I did that?" Well, you're using synthetic data generation. And we're doing the same thing with models. They generate synthetic data, mounds of it. And then from that mound of data, we then can memorize it, basically learn it into a small model. And so that small model could take all this giant amount of data—petabytes and petabytes of data—and it compresses it, encodes it, and learns it into a small model. Because one thing, you know, I've talked for physical AI for a while and people used to say like, "Oh, this is not, you know..." This few years ago, they said, "No, it's not going to happen." Now we're talking about physical AI. But now, especially with cars, it's starting to happen. And I know NVIDIA has announced... We announced this thing called Alpha Mo. It's the world's first reasoning car and it's incredible. You know, we turn on the... you know, "reason out loud." And so we just tell it to reason out loud. It's driving in San Francisco and of course when we tell the car to reason out loud, it just reasons about everything it sees. And so it talks continuously. You don't want a driver... it's like having a chauffeur, just literally everything. "I'm going to stop now. Car coming from the left, I'm going to wait." You don't have to... It's like the mental... it's like a chauffeur that literally tells you everything. Okay, and so we turn on the reasoning mode, it speaks to us and we're driving everywhere and it's reasoning about things. And it's decomposed... "There's a truck parked in the middle of the lane, the other lane, no car is coming, I think I could go to the other side." You know, go around. I mean, now there's a chauffeur, an autonomous vehicle just talking to you continuously. You could tell it to shut up and then we do. But the point is, now we have reasoning systems and the number of diverse environments that it could navigate through is incredible. And so this is really the next, and this is Anirudh's vision. You know, the first time you and I met, Cadence was an EDA company working on electronics and chips and things like that. And you imagined that in fact the future world's going to be full stack. In a lot of ways, you and I see it exactly the same way. It's going to be... if you're an engineer designing a chip, you have to understand the software stack on top, the systems it goes into, the applications of the system beyond that. You have to be a full-stack company because nobody's going to go figure that out for you. How would you even design a chip to do so? And in our case, we're trying to design the system to do so. In your case, you're trying to create tools to enable me to do so. And so both of our companies have to be full-stack companies. And so if that's the case, then Cadence has to move out of the chip into systems, move out of the system into factories. And we're partnered together on AI factories, the world's largest computers. Computer. By the way, the recent results I saw, you know, we have Cadence Reality that simulates and optimizes. And because I think you told us a few months ago one of your big problems is tokens per watt, right? And we worked on that problem and we got... And we are the leaders of low token cost, by the way. I produce the lowest cost tokens in the world. It's a... it's an expensive system, I'll acknowledge that, but it produces so many tokens, it's the lowest cost tokens in the world. It's a great partnership on that. And it's getting better. The more you buy, the more you save. Exactly. Somebody said that, right? If you buy enough of these things, you're going to be rich. You need to have a special... You need to write a book with all your sayings, Jensen, at some point. I crack myself up sometimes. But he is very funny. I'm sure most of you know that, right? He is funny in a lot of... in all the talks you give are pretty funny, and in person. The world is funny to me. You know, you see people do something, you say "That's just funny." You know, why people do... you know why the industry's the way it is, you go, "Well, this is... yeah, that's hilarious. What a dysfunctional group, you know." And so everything is a little funny. But I think... I think... just to ground our conversation and our partnership, I think the... it all started literally with a dinner with Anirudh. And by the way, when Anirudh is excited, that's what he looks like. I'm always excited. After two martinis, that's what he looks like. No change. By the way, you have good taste in martinis. You're right. Monkey 47. Monkey 47. If you have to drink martini, Monkey 47. But... and so... and so it started with us talking about how computing has been reinvented, and how you wanted to reinvent Cadence. And our partnership really evolved from that moment. And I'm really grateful for the work that we're doing together because fundamentally the tools that you create for me enable me to realize my dreams. And so I can't realize my dreams without the Cadence team helping me realize my dreams. And... and this is such an incredible moment because we reinvented computing, that's why NVIDIA is here. And because the EDA industry—the design industry is built on top of computing—now it's reinventing Cadence. And in the short period of time that we've been talking about agents and you've been working on agents, now agents have been realized. People now understand what it means. And so two things I think happened. We reinvented the engineering process of course. But two amazing things happened for Cadence. And this all happened in literally the two years since our martini... and this just tells you how valuable the martini moment is. In that two years—in that two years—Cadence now has an infinite number more chip designers, engineers, that could use Cadence tools. So... so your TAM exploded because of that reason. And second, because you've expanded well beyond chips into the physical world—into where the largest industries in the world are. The chip industry is large, but the rest of the world is giant. And so... and so now Cadence is going to help every company design everything that they make. So congratulations. Good job. Thank you, Jensen. Thank you. Thank you for the partnership. It's a true honor, right? Thank you. Jensen. Anirudh.

WATCH: Trump says he raised tariffs on autos from EU to 25%

PBS NewsHour(5.3M)·21:29·7.5K views·2026-05-01
Donald TrumpPresident
TL;DRPresident Trump announced a 25% tariff on European Union auto imports due to alleged trade deal violations and expressed skepticism toward a new Iranian proposal, citing "disjointed" leadership in Tehran.
Negotiation Status: President Trump confirmed that Iran is seeking...

TL;DR

President Trump announced a 25% tariff on European Union auto imports due to alleged trade deal violations and expressed skepticism toward a new Iranian proposal, citing "disjointed" leadership in Tehran.

Iran and Nuclear Security

  • Negotiation Status: President Trump confirmed that Iran is seeking a deal, but stated he is currently unsatisfied with their proposal.
      • Leadership Discord: He characterized the Iranian leadership as "all messed up" and "disjointed," split between three or four different factions that cannot agree.
  • Military Assessment: Trump claimed the Iranian military has been effectively neutralized.
      • Asset Loss: He stated their navy and air force have been "wiped out" and they currently lack functional radar or anti-aircraft capabilities.
  • Criticism of European Allies: The President expressed dissatisfaction with Italy and Spain regarding their stance on Iran.
      • Nuclear Proliferation: He criticized any nation that would allow Iran to possess a nuclear weapon, calling such a position "not very smart."

Trade and Auto Tariffs

  • EU Tariff Increase: The U.S. has raised tariffs on cars and trucks from the European Union to 25%.
      • Justification: Trump alleged the EU was not adhering to the existing trade agreement.
      • Production Incentives: The administration's goal is to force European manufacturers to move factory production to the United States.
  • Domestic Manufacturing Growth: Trump claimed that over $100 billion is currently being spent on building new car plants within the U.S.
      • Foreign Investment: He noted that companies from Japan, South Korea, Canada, and Mexico are among those currently expanding their U.S. manufacturing footprint.

Diplomatic Relations: Iraq and Pakistan

  • Iraq Leadership: Trump confirmed a phone call with the recent election winner in Iraq, whom he described as a "very good man."
      • U.S. Support: He claimed the victory occurred with "our help" and pledged that the United States is "with him all the way."
  • Pakistan Negotiations: The President expressed "tremendous respect" for the Prime Minister and the Field Marshal of Pakistan.
      • Remote Diplomacy: Due to the length of the trip, negotiations are currently being handled "telephonically" rather than by sending an envoy to Islamabad.

Executive Authority and Congress

  • Congressional Approval: When questioned about seeking congressional approval for certain extensions, Trump dismissed the necessity.
      • Constitutional Stance: He argued that such approval has never been sought by previous administrations and claimed that many consider it "totally unconstitutional" to require it.
      • Communication: He maintained that while he does not seek formal approval, the administration remains "in touch" with Congress.

***

Notable Quotes

"Iran wants to make a deal, because they have no military left, essentially. And, they want to make a deal, but I'm not satisfied with it." — Donald Trump
"We raised the tariffs because they were not, as usual, they were not adhering to the agreement that we have... it forces them to move their factory production much faster." — Donald Trump
"The leadership [in Iran] is very disjointed. It's got two to three groups, maybe four... they all want to make a deal, but they're all messed up." — Donald Trump
"We're always in touch with Congress, but nobody's ever sought it [approval] before, nobody's ever asked for it before... Why should we be different?" — Donald Trump
What is the new Iranian proposal, please? So, they want to make a deal, but I'm not satisfied with it, so we'll see what happens. Iran wants to make a deal because they have no military left, essentially. They want to make a deal, but I'm not satisfied with it. Why were you not seeking congressional approval to extend it? Because it's never been sought before. There's been numerous, many, many times, and nobody's ever gotten it before. They consider it totally unconstitutional. But we're always in touch with Congress; nobody's ever sought it before, nobody's ever asked for it before, and it's never been used before. Why should we be different? Are you willing to send anybody to Islamabad, your envoy? We have great respect for Pakistan and Islamabad, and tremendous respect for the Prime Minister and the Field Marshal. They're working with us, they continue to work with us, but the trip is a very long one. We're doing everything in terms of negotiating right now telephonically. They've made strides, but I'm not sure if they ever get there. There's tremendous discord; they're having a tremendous problem getting along with each other in Iran. The leadership is very disjointed. It's got two to three groups, maybe four, and it's a very disjointed leadership. With that being said, they all want to make a deal, but they're all messed up. I spoke with the winner, a very good man. As you know, I was very strongly in favor of him in Iraq. And we had a long conversation yesterday; I congratulated him. He won, and with our help he won, and we want him to do very well. I told him that the United States is with him all the way; it was a great victory. The new head of Iraq is somebody that we support very strongly. Pakistan has opened the Banzav I was with Iran. You see, can you call me before opening it up to Iran, and are you aware of it? There's a vicious—be, yeah. I know everything about it, yeah. I have great respect. I have great respect for Pakistan and for the Field Marshal and for the Prime Minister. Yeah, I know of it. You spoke with the leader of the US yesterday; you talk about Italy and the withdrawal of troops from Europe, from Italy and Spain. Can you clarify your words? Well, I'm not happy with Italy and I'm not happy with Spain. They feel it's okay for Iran to have a nuclear weapon. Anybody that feels it's okay for Iran to have a nuclear weapon is not very smart, and it'll be a terrible thing in the future if they were ever allowed to have a nuclear weapon. You would have problems the likes of which the world has never seen before, and it's not going to happen. And aside from that, right now they have no navy. It's been wiped out. No air force. It's been wiped out. They have no anything. They have no anti-aircraft, they have no radar. They have no leaders because frankly, their leaders are very disjointed. They have a lot of problems right now. They're not getting along with each other. Their leaders are not getting along with each other, so. Why, why? Right? We raised the tariffs on cars coming in from the European Union because the European Union was not adhering to the trade deal we have. So based on that, now, when they build their plants, of which they're spending over $100 billion for countries, not just the union, there won't be any tariffs. And when those plants open there won't be any tariffs. But we raised the tariffs because they were not, as usual, they were not adhering to the agreement that we have. We have a trade deal with the European Union, they were not adhering to it, so I raised the tariffs on cars and trucks to 25%. That's billions of dollars coming into the United States and it forces them to move their factory production much faster. We have right now in the United States over $100 billion of car plants being built; it's a record. We've never had anything like it from all countries: Japan, South Korea, every—by the way, Canada, Mexico—they're all building plants in the United States. But the European Union was not adhering to the deal that we made. I'll be—I'll move back to my school, if I can sit on here and just kind of go above these guys. Hey, okay. Yeah, I'll get it. Reid Hoffman On The Case For AI Optimism

Prof G Markets(49.2K)·71:41·23.8K views·2026-05-01
Reid HoffmanCo-founder, LinkedIn Scott GallowayHost, Prof G Markets Ed ElsonHost, Prof G Markets
TL;DRReid Hoffman argues that AI represents a "Cognitive Industrial Revolution" where success is measured by technological frontier-building rather than short-term financials; while labor displacement in scripted roles is inevitable, the focus must shift ...
to using AI to...

TL;DR

Reid Hoffman argues that AI represents a "Cognitive Industrial Revolution" where success is measured by technological frontier-building rather than short-term financials; while labor displacement in scripted roles is inevitable, the focus must shift to using AI to democratize essential services like healthcare and education.

The State of OpenAI and the Path to IPO

  • Investment Philosophy: Hoffman, an early investor in OpenAI and a Microsoft board member, dismissed concerns regarding OpenAI missing revenue and user targets.
      • Prioritizing Frontier Models: He argues that the quality of technology (e.g., the 5.5 release) is the leading indicator for future growth, regardless of current quarter-over-quarter reliability.
      • Public Market Scrutiny: Hoffman compares AI companies to early Amazon or Tesla—entities that trade on a "foundational promise" of the future rather than traditional P/E ratios.
  • Unit Economics: The industry is currently in a race to an "asymptote" where the exponential cost of training models eventually meets the physical limits of power (gigawatts) and capital.
      • Inference vs. Training: While training costs are skyrocketing, the economics for "inference" (running the models for users) are already becoming viable and attractive.

The Global Competitive Landscape

  • Tiered Rankings: Hoffman identifies a distinct hierarchy among the major players:
      • Tier 1 (The Leaders): OpenAI and Anthropic currently hold the strongest positions in terms of frontier capabilities.
      • Tier 2 (Big Tech): Google (Gemini) is the primary challenger, followed by Meta and Microsoft’s internal efforts.
      • The "Glengarry Glen Ross" Market: In specialized fields like coding, Hoffman notes a "winner-takes-most" dynamic. Currently, Claude Code (Anthropic) leads in interaction, while OpenAI Codex leads in long-form reasoning.
  • The China Factor: Chinese models like ByteDance’s multimodal video tools and the Qwen open-source model are highly credible but often rely on "distillation" (learning from Western frontier models). Hoffman believes future distillation will become significantly harder.
  • Apple's Lag: Hoffman noted that Apple appears behind the curve, remarking that "they still don't seem to realize that Siri is like 20 years old in tech terms."

AI Risks: Cybersecurity and Labor

  • Mythos and the "Crime Scene" Computer: Hoffman addressed Anthropic's warnings about "Mythos" (their latest agentic capability).
      • Automated Penetration Testing: AI allows for an "infinite" number of virtual cybersecurity engineers. This fundamentally shifts the landscape by allowing rogue actors to probe billions of lines of legacy code that were previously too expensive to attack.
  • The Cognitive Industrial Revolution: Hoffman admits the transition will be painful, specifically for "scripted" labor.
      • High-Risk Sectors: Customer service jobs are expected to "go completely away," and sales roles will undergo massive structural shifts.
      • Historical Parallel: Much like the Industrial Revolution eliminated "horse-cleanup jobs" but created the automotive industry, AI will create new, currently unpredictable roles.
      • Transition Strategy: Hoffman advocates for deploying AI *for* the transition—using it to help displaced workers find new roles and acquire new skills.

Social Responsibility and the "80% Solution"

  • Democratizing Expertise: Hoffman suggests that governments should negotiate deals with AI labs to provide "free-at-point-of-use" AI assistants on every smartphone.
      • Essential Services: These would include a medical assistant, a legal assistant, and a tutor for every citizen to offset the economic "melee" of the transition.
  • Wealth Inequality vs. Prosperity: Hoffman is less concerned with "inequality" as a metric and more focused on whether the "bulk of 80% of society" sees their circumstances improve.
      • Progressive Taxation: While he supports progressive taxation to fix social dysfunctions, he argues it is not a sufficient solution on its own without fundamental economic renovation.

Legal Battles and Market Speculation

  • Elon Musk vs. OpenAI: Hoffman characterized Musk’s lawsuit against OpenAI as "legal harassment" and "factually incorrect." He claims Musk attempted to take 80% ownership of the company and left when the board refused, only to sue later when the organization succeeded.
  • SpaceX IPO Rumors: Hoffman expressed skepticism regarding a potential SpaceX IPO, suggesting that some investors may be buying in based on "Bitcoin-style" momentum rather than sane investing behavior. He noted rumors that Musk might eventually attempt to merge SpaceX with Tesla to bolster market cap.

***

Notable Quotes

"I tend to think a little bit on the coding side... it’s a Glengarry Glen Ross market: first prize Cadillac, second prize steak knives, third prize you're fired." — Reid Hoffman
"One of the things that's been a bit of a social problem over the last few decades has been that so much of the growth has been contained only to the private markets... I think that's one of the benefits of having some of these AI companies go public." — Reid Hoffman
"The Industrial Age was really painful in terms of its transition... I think the same thing is true of the Cognitive Industrial Revolution with AI, but the transition is really painful. How do we make the transition better? The simplest thing is: deploy AI for it." — Reid Hoffman
.001. That's the percentage of deep-sea ocean floor that explorers have actually seen. Ed, how do you identify the blind man at a nudist beach? How? It's not hard, Ed. Ed, how are you? Let's see. I'm headed to Florence, Italy. What? For what? For a speaking gig. Hold the phone. Now we know why my speaking revenue has crashed. You're taking my gigs. Let me get this. Who's bringing you to Florence? Royal Bank of Canada. RBC is bringing you to Florence? That's right. Florence, baby. I'll be there for one day, so I'll give that, do that, probably try to have some fun as much as I can, but we'll be recording at the same time, coming back, then going to Texas. Oh, well, that should be easy. There's a ton of direct flights from Florence to Houston. Oh my god, you're literally living the life I had in the 20s and 30s, and let me tell you, it's exhausting. Look at me, you're going to look like this. I'm anxious about it, for sure. So you are now getting my speaking gigs and note to self: undermine Ed's credibility, begin slowly diminishing his professional standing, kill the prince. Okay, okay. That's great, congratulations. Thank you. Yeah, I'm excited. It'll be fun. That's really exciting. Have you been to Florence before? No, it's going to be my first time. It's I love the tourist places. People always say, "Oh, it's so touristy," like Venice and Florence. I love highly touristy destinations. I think they're great. It's a beautiful little city. Why are you a fan of the tourist traps? I went to Florence right out of college with a backpack and a Eurail pass. Nice. Do young people do that anymore, or do you just sit at home on your phones? Yeah, of course. Interrailing. That's what I did too. Yeah, a little bit. I joined my friends for the latter half, so I was mostly in Eastern Europe. We were in like Slovenia, which was not that interesting. But Florence would have been fun. Even if I'll probably have like an hour to myself, if I had to see one thing in Florence, do you have any recommendations? The honest answer is just get fucked up and eat a lot of good food. I don't have any like cultural landmarks or "in the know" in Florence with Scott Galloway. I couldn't be less Stanley Tucci. I couldn't be. I'm like, "Where's the bar? Where's the bar?" Yeah, that's not my gig, but I'm very happy for you. Should—oh, we should get on our promo. Ed, how are ticket sales going? What's going on with our tour? I believe they're going quite well. We're—I think we're close to sold out in San Francisco. To be honest, I haven't checked, so I'm sort of speaking out of my ass here. New York and San Francisco are almost sold out. We have—very soon we're going to be announcing speakers in LA, Miami, and I think—are we close to New York? I think we're close. Very excited about that. We're doing kind of business of entertainment or business of nightlife in Miami. We're going to do business of money or business of finance in New York. We're going to do business of media/entertainment in LA, business of tech in San Francisco. You know, that kind of stuff. But if you are interested in getting a ticket, please go to ProfGMarketsTour.com. Prof G Markets Tour. Thank you. ProfGMarketsTour.com, and we look to see you in either LA, San Francisco, Chicago, Miami, or New York. Absolutely. And if you're watching us on YouTube and you like what you're hearing, hit subscribe. And if you're listening on Spotify or Apple Podcasts, hit follow. And with that, shall we get into a conversation? Let's do it. Over the past month, we have focused on the downsides of AI, from real-world violence targeting industry leaders to growing political pushback against data center expansion. So this felt like the right moment to bring on someone who believes that AI can serve the public good. Our guest has spent years making the case that AI will improve our lives. He has also created, advised, and invested in some of the largest and most successful technology companies in the world. Few individuals sit closer to what might turn out to be the most transformative technology of our time. And so we want to find out what is actually happening on the ground. Here is our conversation with Reid Hoffman, co-founder of LinkedIn, Inflection, and Manas AI, partner at Greylock, and author of "Superagency: What Could Possibly Go Right with Our AI Future". Reid, great to have you on the show. Thank you for joining us. I want to start with some specific news that we saw this week that is making investors a little bit anxious, and that is this OpenAI news, specifically that they missed their revenue target last year. They also missed their user target, and they're obviously set to go public soon. It should be one of the most important IPOs ever, and suddenly investors are very anxious about this company. They are anxious that they're actually not growing in the way that they had expected. And you are in an interesting position because one, you're on the board of Microsoft, and Microsoft is obviously one of the largest shareholders in OpenAI, one of the earliest investors, and also you are an early investor in OpenAI through your VC firm Greylock. So let's just start with this. What do you make of this news, and should investors be worried about OpenAI? Well, ultimately, as an investor, I'm not worried. I mean, part of it is the company had very aggressive targets, and so when you have aggressive targets and you kind of go in a little below them, that's actually not the kind of thing that I worry about that much. And classically, the reason why public companies—because you know, most public investors tend to want reliability quarter to quarter—you tend to do your best to be on target in terms of what they're saying because there's this kind of reliability thing. Whereas I as a private investor tend to be the "just establish the really, really strong basis". And so you know, for me, the thing I wasn't tracking so much was last year's revenue or last year's user count. Those are great, making progress, obviously we'll need to grow more too, but a lot more. But the real question that I was like very happy to see was the 5.5 release, because I think the thing that I'm most tracking is whether OpenAI is continuing to deliver some of the world's best technology, frontier models and so forth. And you know, that's I think the precursor to everything else, and that from every source I've seen and multiple different benchmarks and multiple different engagements has been the kind of new world leader in quality of model. So that is more of what I pay attention to because it's—you know, with investing, it's downstream effects. It's: what does next year look like? That is the most relevant question. Does that make you concerned? It's an interesting point. Like early-stage investors, venture capitalists, they're more interested in the technology, they're more interested in what's going to happen 10, 15, 20 years down the line. Public markets investors are more interested in what's happening right now. I mean, we're literally reading quarterly earnings, we look at what happened last year, we look at what happened in the previous quarter. It's much more of a sort of backward-looking practice, which seems to be like it might be a big problem for OpenAI if they are to go public at the end of this year because suddenly they will be scrutinized for their financials. And so I wonder if that concerns you that suddenly the company is leaving an ecosystem of people and investors that are okay with financials that might not make sense right now, but then suddenly they're going and pitching the company to a group of investors who care a lot about that stuff. Are you worried that maybe they will get punished or that they will have a hard time in the public markets when that time comes? Well, if they can't establish themselves, there is a small set of companies—and it isn't just Tesla, which is probably the extreme example of this, which is kind of betting on the future—but Amazon for a significant number of years, and others that say, "Hey, I've got a foundational promise of where I'm going" where investors go, "We believe in that future, and so we're buying in that future." And the fact that the current P/E comparables and other kinds of things don't make sense on a quarter-by-quarter basis, even factoring in an interesting CAGR or anything else. I think it'll be for all of the AI companies that are kind of prepping and considering going public, it'll be important to establish that basis because I think the first year or two will look a lot like early dot-com companies, some of which were complete flameouts and some of which were enduring institutions, Amazon being an example. What do you make of the unit economics right now? Because this is something I've been thinking about when we think about these companies like 10 years down the line where currently the unit economics don't really make sense. Like the cost to build these models is so incredibly high, and that is why OpenAI and Anthropic, any of the sort of foundation labs, are burning a lot of money right now. It almost seems as if the only way that this works out from the perspective of the business model is for these companies to become like essentially utility companies, like almost have these monopolies over compute. And Sam Altman has said this; he has said that eventually he could see OpenAI becoming something like a utility company down the line, which to me is quite an interesting perspective. Because it's quite different from what we saw in the dot-com boom where we've had, you know, a handful of companies—we might call it an oligopoly—but a handful of companies operating in the same space. I mean, where do you see the business model trending further down the line? Obviously, you know, as you know, you have to distinguish between the training costs and the inference costs. And one of the things when you look at these numbers is that the inference costs are actually in fact pretty good economics for inference, but obviously you have an exponentiating training category. And so the question is, as you're doing this exponentiating training—and I do think that at some point the training costs, even though there's a goal to get as strong as you can there because then your quality of model kind of dominates through all the different inference units. Essentially, at some level of exponential, that starts asymptoting just because it doesn't make sense within, you know, how many gigawatts do we have? What kind of economic cost do we get there? And I think a little bit of the race is a race to that asymptote. Now, where exactly that asymptote is, I think is somewhat governed by availability of capital from investors believing, and then the delivery of revenue. Now, part of the delivery of revenue is not just the current services, where you say, "Okay, you know, OpenAI is dominating the chatbot and kind of what the consumer interaction with that service is." Anthropic is doing the coding side, followed by OpenAI on the coding side in terms of how the API and coding works. But like, this has got to be just the beginning. Part of how you're looking at these AI models is not just the provision of tokens, which obviously is interesting across a number of things, but what is the output that comes from? Now that software engineering can be much more broadly spread in a whole bunch of areas which otherwise wouldn't have been able to afford software engineering, it can now do them. That can affect the productivity of their areas. What happens there? What happens in services firms when a bunch of different services—take legal or accounting or anything else—can be done with intensive amplification of this? It's not just a question of, you know, paying seats in a software basis, but what is the delivery in terms of the margins of what the delivery of the service is? And where all those economics play out is I think very early in TBD. And you'd say, "Well, should we wait to go public for those?" I actually think one of the things when you—you two know this better than I do—but I think one of the things that's been a bit of a kind of call it a social problem over the last few decades has been that so much of the growth has been contained only to the private markets because of like, let's delay going public until it's extremely stable. Which means that a lot of folks who cannot participate in the private markets only get exposure to the public markets. And I think that's one of the benefits of having some of these AI companies. But I think it's early relative to what is the business model going to be and then what is that revenue going to be. And now utility, I think in terms of the fact that any task that you do with language or information, I think will have AI participation at some level of depth, whether it's complete automation, whether it's augmentation, whether it's assistance in various ways. And so the spread of that is I think a good measure on utility. I think one of the things that's good for society is the fact that we have multiple providers of these frontier models, so they're competing with each other in terms of pricing, in terms of availability to entrepreneurs building other kinds of different services, in terms of being able to—you know, like the best governance mechanism is, you know, relative to the health of society for the cost of things. Oligopoly, monopoly, etc., is to have competition which then brings the price down, and I think that's something that we are also seeing. So the utility question around kind of around the—like should it run like utility?—you know, I don't know how well utilities are thought of in the UK. In the US, utilities are kind of a disaster. So you don't want it run like that. So I'd love to just get your—I mean granted you might have a bias because you're on the board of Microsoft, but and you're an investor in several AI platforms or LLMs—can you give us a lay of the land in terms of the competitive set? You know, obviously OpenAI, Anthropic, Google with Gemini, and then the Chinese models and I'm sure there's a bunch of others, but give us what you think is the competitive landscape right now. So I think—and this is to some degree good news for startups and productivity startups—I think the strongest positions are OpenAI and Anthropic. I think in the traditional big companies, Gemini is next, and I think will have a bunch of different efforts from Meta, Microsoft on its own, obviously used a lot of OpenAI right now, and Amazon. Who knows where Apple will play out in all of this stuff since, you know, they still don't seem to realize that Siri is like 20 years old kind of in tech terms. Now for the Chinese models, the interesting question will be like—there's a lot of good open-source models that pretty clearly have some roots in distillation from kind of the major Western frontier models. And I think part of what we're going into is an area where that distillation will get a lot harder. Now, that being said, the Chinese are building up compute, building up chips, have extraordinary amounts of talent, hardworking, great tech companies. Matter of fact, the SeaDance multimodal video model is I think amongst the best in the world. So I think it's already, you know, you already have stuff that's kind of playing there. But like and then you kind of have a jagged edge. Like you go, "Okay, let's go coding-specific." So most developers will say Claude Code. Part of the reason is because Claude Code has got the best model, I think the best interaction thing for iterating through kind of the amplification of a software engineer. But then the in-depth engineers that I know who play with them go OpenAI Codex because it's much more useful at like call it 20, 30, 50, 90 minute reasoning long tasks that play to harder engineering, and so they prefer that versus Claude Code. And right now, at the moment, that's it when you get to like people who have exposure to it. Now some people then go, "Okay, I can't afford either of those, so I'll use Qwen," you know, the the Chinese open-source model, which is quite credible, but when you think about the fact that when you're looking for coding, just as one instance, you're actually looking for something that's quite good and quite reliable that doesn't introduce bugs. Obviously, we have Mythos coming with questions around cybersecurity and what does this all mean there, and that matters too. So the I tend to think a little bit on the coding side, for your particular coding problem, it's a little bit like what we call in blitzscaling a Glengarry Glen Ross market, which is, you know, first prize Cadillac, second prize steak knives, third prize you're fired. So roughly. But then it's different across, you know, like images—the OpenAI image generator is I think now like—like I've been wanting to create graphic novels for eight years with AI, and I now think I can. So that's interesting. Anyway, we could spend the entire time on the different capabilities of these. So it's a good segue into talking about Mythos. So Anthropic described Mythos; he said that it turns every computer into a crime scene. And I'm trying to distinguish between what is responsible warnings about their own product and catastrophizing as a means of fundraising. Because everyone's talking about Mythos right now and there's sort of this—and it's prevalent I think across a lot of AI—that my technology is so amazing that it's going to reshape the world both good and bad. Please invest in my Series D at 100 times revenues. How much of this is responsible warnings from, you know, Dr. Frankenstein that Frank could be dangerous, and how much of it is just quite frankly is just fundraising? One, I think the Anthropic people are very earnest, principled, and honest. So I think there's a fundamental basis which is completely honest. And here's a simple way of making that case, which is if you said the minimum that Mythos is is an ability to have an infinite number of quality cybersecurity engineers who are penetration testers—and it's not infinite obviously because there's a compute cost for running them, but you could take a thousand of them and run them in a direction—you end up even if it's just that, you end up with a re-changing of the cybersecurity landscape. Because we have billions of lines of code that essentially haven't been touched, and partially because there hasn't been either an economic model for the number of cybercriminals or the number of people employed by rogue states to go after any other than a certain set of systems. But it broadens the range by the ability to just kind of spin up many new penetration testing engineers with a kind of an AI mechanism. And that's the minimum that I think you could look at the discussion around Mythos and its cybersecurity issues. I think that it's actually somewhat better than that, and I don't mean that that's amazing, what I just said. But I don't know yet how much more. Right? Like is it "Oh my gosh, you put a top 10% cybersecurity engineer and put them against, you know, a couple of Mythos agents, and do they outpace that person?" It's unclear to me. Does it think in ways that we haven't thought of before and generate new patterns? That's unclear to me. But you don't need those to have this be a massive kind of shift in how we think about cybersecurity, what are the kinds of things we need to do. And so I actually think that they're doing a responsible thing by saying, "There's an important change coming, and we need to get at least the essential systems ready." We'll grant you more than a minute to answer this because it's a difficult—or you know, we're going to need a bigger boat. But Andrew Yang, who was on our podcast a few days ago, said, you know, he's having a moment, right? Because he's sort of in some ways predicted all the fears, the sum of all fears around capital destroying labor. And there's already evidence around youth unemployment increasing, a lack of hiring, layoffs at some of the early adopters. We—I won't use "we"—I have taken the view that we have been to this movie before and some of the catastrophizing around the destruction in the labor market doesn't recognize the new opportunities and the new jobs that'll be created. So I would love to just get your thought and take as whatever time you need to describe what you think the impact on the labor market is going to be of AI. Every job that uses language or information will have an AI component that will range in certain cases of automation, in certain cases much more AI work, and in certain cases kind of AI amplification or augmentation of human labor. And I think that will be true of all of those things. And so almost every industry, because if you think about like even steel manufacture, you still have meetings, financial analysis, capital, legal, strategic planning, and so forth. All that plays into it. So that means that AI will touch everything. Now, and I by the way inherently also agree with you that one of the general problems when you get new technology—I kind of put this in superagency—is you go, "Well, I can see if I take jobs as a fixed number that do not grow and do not change, and I can see that a bunch of them will be gone." "Now the horses that carry the buggies will go away and we'll have cars, and like all the groomspeople will go away and all the horse carrying jobs will go away and all the horse cleanup jobs will go away." I can tell that and be like, "Well, but you didn't predict all the new cars, the new jobs and drivers and blah blah blah in that particular thing and you can predict the negative changes and you can't predict the positive ones." So now the rub in this is there's a couple of places where AI creates kind of multi-industry, very fast, much cheaper things. Like say for example if you said, "Well, you know, like for example one of the areas where I think you'll see a lot of automation is where human beings are following a script." The AI will follow a script much better. So I think customer service jobs go completely away. But by the way, I think like sales jobs transfer a whole bunch too, and what does the new sales universe look like?
